EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms moved over Iowa, Illinois, and northern Missouri from the early morning hours through the late evening hours. Many locations saw damaging winds during this multiple round event. with locations near the city of Cedar Rapids seeing a very large hail event.

Widespread severe thunderstorms from the late morning through the evening hours June 20th, produced large hail, damaging winds, and heavy rains.  In addition 4 tornadoes occurred.  One tornado resulted in 2 injuries when a house was destroyed.  Another person was injured by a lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey found a tornado touched down 2.5 miles southeast of Mount Pleasant and remained on the ground for 3.45 miles before lifting 2.67 miles southwest of New London. The tornado was rated EF-1 with peak winds estimated at 105 MPH. The damage was primarily to farm outbuildings and trees. Two people were injured when a mobile home was rolled over by the tornado.

A winter storm hit northeast Iowa on December 28th and 29th with heavy snow, sleet and strong winds.  As the storm lifted north out of the Southern Plains states, snow moved into northeast Iowa during the morning of the 28th. For parts of northeast Iowa, the snow was mixed with or completely changed over to sleet during the afternoon and early evening of the 28th. The heaviest snow fell during the late afternoon and early evening of the 28th with 1 to 2 inch per hour rates. This caused a rapid deterioration in road conditions causing travel conditions to quickly become dangerous and some county sheriff departments recommended no travel.  By the time the snow ended on the 29th, totals across northeast Iowa ranged from 6 to 11 inches. The highest reported total was 11.5 inches in Monona (Clayton County). Significant blowing and drifting of the snow occurred as winds gusted into the 25 to 40 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 6 to 8 inches of snow across Mitchell County. The highest reported total was 8.5 inches northwest of St. Ansgar. Winds gusting into the 35 to 40 mph range created considerable blowing and drifting snow.

Winds gusting into the 30 to 35 mph range created considerable blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 7 inches in the Spirit Lake to Storm Lake areas of northwest Iowa from early morning to early afternoon of February 25th. Lesser snow accumulations were reported further west in northwest Iowa. The snow was accompanied by northeast winds gusting to 35 mph over the warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches. The snow was accompanied by northeast winds gusting to 30 mph, causing areas of blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow spread across the Midwest the afternoon of January 5, 2015, with accumulations in Eastern Iowa beginning mid afternoon, and spreading into Northern Illinois mid evening. Widespread 4 to 7 inch amounts were reported, with numerous school cancellations on January 6th. Due to the cold air in place, the snow was fluffy in nature, but with little wind, blowing snow was not an issue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported around 4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure, associated with record or near record surface pressure, settled into the mid Missouri River Valley during the early morning on the 7th. The combination of arctic air with actual air temperatures from 5 below to 5 above zero, and winds of 15 to 30 mph with gusts to near 40 mph created wind chill values of 15 to 25 below zero starting in the early morning on the 7th continuing through the early afternoon in many areas.

The main impact of the storm was to cause difficulties in weekend travel for those who did not delay their travel plans. The storm began on January 31st and continued into the new month through much of February 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 7 inches. Accompanying northwest winds gusting to 35 mph caused areas of blowing snow, reducing visibility to 1/4 mile at a few places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 7 inches in the Spirit Lake to Storm Lake areas of northwest Iowa from early morning to early afternoon of February 25th. Lesser snow accumulations were reported further west in northwest Iowa. The snow was accompanied by northeast winds gusting to 35 mph over the warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 6 inches, including 4 inches at Spencer. The snow was accompanied by northeast winds gusting to 30 mph which caused areas of blowing snow, reducing visibility to 1/4 to 1/2 mile at Spencer Airport for at least three straight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought heavy rains to northeast Iowa in the middle of December. Temperatures with this system remained above freezing allowing all the precipitation to fall as rain. Amounts ranged from a little over an inch to almost 5 inches from Winneshiek and Fayette Counties east to the Mississippi River. The highest reported rainfall total in this portion of northeast Iowa was 4.84 inches in Eldorado (Fayette County). This rain caused in bank rises to occur on most rivers and streams along with minor river flooding along the Volga and Turkey Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Volga River out of its banks in Littleport. The river crested at 15.42 feet, almost three and a half feet over the flood stage.

A slow moving thunderstorms moving across Page County produced 2 to 4 inches of rainfall during the evening of July 15th. Additional thunderstorms occurred during the overnight hours with storm total precipitation measured at over 5 inches in parts of the county. This led to mainly urban flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported extensive street flooding in the city of Clarinda. Up to two feet of water was reported in some streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused damaging winds at several locations in northwest Iowa from just before to a little after sunrise on the morning of June 22nd. There was also one report of penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds destroyed an airport hangar, and destroyed or damaged 5 planes in the hangar. The winds caused widespread tree damage, including numerous trees blown down or completely uprooted. The tree damage included many trees at a local golf course. The winds damaged homes and buildings in and near Sheldon, including one home that was damaged by a large falling tree. A large silo was damaged and several small farm buildings were destroyed. The farm damage included a large shed destroyed and machinery inside the shed damaged. A commercial building under construction was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced damaging winds at Little Rock in Lyon County of far northwest Iowa on the evening of June 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including several large trees blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly northward moving boundary/warm front was draped across the area throughout the day.  By the evening vigorous storms fired up just north of the boundary and continued as the low level jet began to pick up.  Numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Fairly quickly after sunset, storms transitioned away from multi-cellular severe to training heavy rain producers over an area NW to SE roughly from Carroll to Bloomfield, including portions of the Des Moines metro.  Resulting rainfall totals exceeded 3 inches in many locations and a few eclipsing 5 and 6 inches.  Widespread flash flooding and eventually river flooding occurred across large portions of the area mentioned above. Precipitable water values were in the 1.5 to 2.2 inch range with the freezing level around 14 to 15 kft across the state throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported heavy rainfall of 2.88 inches over the last 24 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including up to 2 foot diameter trees snapped. The winds also blew down power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front produced areas of thunderstorms with large hail up to the size of quarters and frequent lightning.  One person was killed and another injured by lightning in Muscatine County.  Heavy rain also was common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Muscatine County Emergency Manager reported a male, age 36, was struck by lightning and killed while riding a horse.  Another male, age 25, was injured.  The horse was also killed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system lifted northeast out of the southwest U.S. during the early morning hours of the 17th.  The surface low was over South Dakota as a line of thunderstorms developed ahead of an advancing dry line that was south of the low.  The atmosphere was moderately unstable with about 2000 J/kg as the storms developed in the evening.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was only about 400 J/kg with a freezing level around 13,000 feet.  The lifted index was in the -4 to -5 C range as the storms approached with 40 to 45 kts of shear available.  The LCL was quite low, around 750 meters.  CAPE in the zero to 3 km layer was 50 to 100 J/kg.  Precipitable water values were quite high, in the 1.25 to 1.5 inch range.  For the most part, the thunderstorms were not severe and produced mainly heavy rainfall.  Several reports of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall were received from southwest into north central Iowa.  As the line advanced eastward, a small mesoscale vorticity maximum was observed riding north along the line.  The thunderstorms became enhanced as it moved north with a line of damage extending from Clarke County north to Webster County.  Eighty two cars of a railroad train were derailed by the winds, with two of them blown off into the nearby field.  Numerous very large trees were downed and some farm buildings lost roofs.  Reports of trees and power lines being blown down continued north as the vorticity maximum lifted north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused tree and power line damage west of Ogden.

A couple of funnel cloud reports were associated with a few weak storms that formed  across central Iowa during the late afternoon. Zero to 3 km CAPE was very strong at around 150 to 200 J/kg with some surface vorticity in place. LCLs were low around 750 m and the Non-Supercell Tornado parameter had a 2 around south central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter and public reports of a funnel cloud.  Relayed via Marshall County Dispatch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped through the area with a large MCS developing across central Iowa throughout the overnight hours and continued into the early morning hours of the 15th. Storms continued to track over the same areas resulting in heavy rainfall across large portions of central Iowa. Flash flooding was reported in several areas of Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported flooded intersection due to heavy rainfall and rapid runoff.  Road blocked after reported inundation at NW College and NW 4th streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the state during the day, dragging behind it a cold front that helped trigger off storms across portions of central and southern Iowa. Hail, damaging winds and a tornado occurred with the storms. The tornado moved from Monroe County into Wapello County damaging multiple farmsteads, power poles and a house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports strong low level rotation and a brief funnel cloud approximately 2 miles southeast of Eddyville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system lifted northeast out of the southwest U.S. during the early morning hours of the 17th.  The surface low was over South Dakota as a line of thunderstorms developed ahead of an advancing dry line that was south of the low.  The atmosphere was moderately unstable with about 2000 J/kg as the storms developed in the evening.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was only about 400 J/kg with a freezing level around 13,000 feet.  The lifted index was in the -4 to -5 C range as the storms approached with 40 to 45 kts of shear available.  The LCL was quite low, around 750 meters.  CAPE in the zero to 3 km layer was 50 to 100 J/kg.  Precipitable water values were quite high, in the 1.25 to 1.5 inch range.  For the most part, the thunderstorms were not severe and produced mainly heavy rainfall.  Several reports of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall were received from southwest into north central Iowa.  As the line advanced eastward, a small mesoscale vorticity maximum was observed riding north along the line.  The thunderstorms became enhanced as it moved north with a line of damage extending from Clarke County north to Webster County.  Eighty two cars of a railroad train were derailed by the winds, with two of them blown off into the nearby field.  Numerous very large trees were downed and some farm buildings lost roofs.  Reports of trees and power lines being blown down continued north as the vorticity maximum lifted north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This short-lived tornado damaged a few trees along its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough pushed through the northern plains with an associated cold front extending southward into the central plains. Storms fired along the front to the west of Iowa in the evening and developed into a large MCS as it moved into Iowa. An MCV and a large squall line moved through northern and central Iowa during the overnight hours producing strong, damaging winds and heavy rain. Four brief tornadoes also occurred in central Iowa along the leading edge of the squall line in an area of stronger shear and higher moisture. The tornadoes mainly damaged trees and crops, however one did strike a barn and silo in rural Boone County. Another damaged traffic signals and an Iowa DNR nursery in the Ames area of Story County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured on a personal weather station.

CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was only about 400 J/kg with a freezing level around 13,000 feet.  The lifted index was in the -4 to -5 C range as the storms approached with 40 to 45 kts of shear available.  The LCL was quite low, around 750 meters.  CAPE in the zero to 3 km layer was 50 to 100 J/kg.  Precipitable water values were quite high, in the 1.25 to 1.5 inch range.  For the most part, the thunderstorms were not severe and produced mainly heavy rainfall.  Several reports of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall were received from southwest into north central Iowa.  As the line advanced eastward, a small mesoscale vorticity maximum was observed riding north along the line.  The thunderstorms became enhanced as it moved north with a line of damage extending from Clarke County north to Webster County.  Eighty two cars of a railroad train were derailed by the winds, with two of them blown off into the nearby field.  Numerous very large trees were downed and some farm buildings lost roofs.  Reports of trees and power lines being blown down continued north as the vorticity maximum lifted north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on houses in town and power was out.  Relayed by KCCI TV.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly northward moving boundary/warm front was draped across the area throughout the day.  By the evening vigorous storms fired up just north of the boundary and continued as the low level jet began to pick up.  Numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Fairly quickly after sunset, storms transitioned away from multi-cellular severe to training heavy rain producers over an area NW to SE roughly from Carroll to Bloomfield, including portions of the Des Moines metro.  Resulting rainfall totals exceeded 3 inches in many locations and a few eclipsing 5 and 6 inches.  Widespread flash flooding and eventually river flooding occurred across large portions of the area mentioned above. Precipitable water values were in the 1.5 to 2.2 inch range with the freezing level around 14 to 15 kft across the state throughout the event.

A warm front was draped across the region on the afternoon and evening of June 10th with weak shear and a large amount of CAPE. Thunderstorms developed across the area producing numerous hail reports, heavy rain, and a few high winds across southern and southwest Iowa. Two funnel clouds were also noted in south central Iowa.

An MCS rolled through northern Iowa in the AM hours bringing with it numerous reports of high/damaging winds and some hail. The damaging winds brought down large tree limbs, power lines, large trees and damaged chicken and hog facilities. Lightning struck a house in Kossuth County damaging the chimney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported numerous six to eight inch tree limbs down.  Time radar estimated.

An upper level trough pushed through the northern plains with an associated cold front extending southward into the central plains. Storms fired along the front to the west of Iowa in the evening and developed into a large MCS as it moved into Iowa. An MCV and a large squall line moved through northern and central Iowa during the overnight hours producing strong, damaging winds and heavy rain. Four brief tornadoes also occurred in central Iowa along the leading edge of the squall line in an area of stronger shear and higher moisture. The tornadoes mainly damaged trees and crops, however one did strike a barn and silo in rural Boone County. Another damaged traffic signals and an Iowa DNR nursery in the Ames area of Story County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four houses with tree damage and some outbuilding damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped through the area with a large MCS developing across central Iowa throughout the overnight hours and continued into the early morning hours of the 15th. Storms continued to track over the same areas resulting in heavy rainfall across large portions of central Iowa. Flash flooding was reported in several areas of Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS reported heavy rainfall of 2.29 inches over the last 24 hours.  CoCoRaHS station Clive 0.1 WSW - IA-PK-75.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports two funnel clouds northwest of Clearfield.  No contact with the ground was made by the funnel clouds at the time of the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line moved across northeast Iowa on June 22nd. This squall line produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and at least one tornado. The tornado destroyed one farm house near Maynard (Fayette County) causing injuries to both occupants of the house. Several other nearby farms sustained damage to crops, trees, and barns.  As the squall line moved across northeast Iowa it produced a swath of wind damage starting near Marble Rock (Floyd County) to around Guttenberg (Clayton County). Within this swath, a 65 mph wind gust was measured in Marble Rock that took down trees and power lines, around 17 farms sustained damage across southern Fayette County, and an Amish grocery store was destroyed near Garber (Clayton County). Preliminary damage estimates for Fayette County are in excess of $3 million. Locally heavy rains also produced some flash flooding with 480th Street washed out south of Stacyville (Floyd County) and Waterloo Creek came out of its banks near Dorchester (Allamakee County) within 30 minutes of the rain starting. The State of Iowa Governor issued a disaster proclamation for the following counties: Allamakee, Clayton, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek. A federal disaster declaration was made for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Clayton, Fayette, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado started about 4 miles southeast of Maynard and ended approximately 5 miles southeast of Maynard. The tornado destroyed a farmhouse by moving it off its foundation and it then fell into the basement. Two people inside the house sustained non-life threatening injuries. The tornado also damaged crops, trees and farm buildings.

As the line advanced eastward, a small mesoscale vorticity maximum was observed riding north along the line.  The thunderstorms became enhanced as it moved north with a line of damage extending from Clarke County north to Webster County.  Eighty two cars of a railroad train were derailed by the winds, with two of them blown off into the nearby field.  Numerous very large trees were downed and some farm buildings lost roofs.  Reports of trees and power lines being blown down continued north as the vorticity maximum lifted north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Farm buildings were damaged or destroyed in the 1675 to 1700 blocks of Highway 34.  A 2 X 10 was driven into the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly northward moving boundary/warm front was draped across the area throughout the day.  By the evening vigorous storms fired up just north of the boundary and continued as the low level jet began to pick up.  Numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Fairly quickly after sunset, storms transitioned away from multi-cellular severe to training heavy rain producers over an area NW to SE roughly from Carroll to Bloomfield, including portions of the Des Moines metro.  Resulting rainfall totals exceeded 3 inches in many locations and a few eclipsing 5 and 6 inches.  Widespread flash flooding and eventually river flooding occurred across large portions of the area mentioned above. Precipitable water values were in the 1.5 to 2.2 inch range with the freezing level around 14 to 15 kft across the state throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water reported over many roads in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was draped across the region on the afternoon and evening of June 10th with weak shear and a large amount of CAPE. Thunderstorms developed across the area producing numerous hail reports, heavy rain, and a few high winds across southern and southwest Iowa. Two funnel clouds were also noted in south central Iowa.

An MCS rolled through northern Iowa in the AM hours bringing with it numerous reports of high/damaging winds and some hail. The damaging winds brought down large tree limbs, power lines, large trees and damaged chicken and hog facilities. Lightning struck a house in Kossuth County damaging the chimney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported winds estimated at 70 mph.  Multiple tree limbs down in the area as well.  The best measured winds were from a personal weather station with poor siting at 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough pushed through the northern plains with an associated cold front extending southward into the central plains. Storms fired along the front to the west of Iowa in the evening and developed into a large MCS as it moved into Iowa. An MCV and a large squall line moved through northern and central Iowa during the overnight hours producing strong, damaging winds and heavy rain. Four brief tornadoes also occurred in central Iowa along the leading edge of the squall line in an area of stronger shear and higher moisture. The tornadoes mainly damaged trees and crops, however one did strike a barn and silo in rural Boone County. Another damaged traffic signals and an Iowa DNR nursery in the Ames area of Story County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall over the previous 24 hours of 2.00 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front and associated surface trough moved through the state and stalled across the southern portions of the state, firing off a few storms in southern/southwest Iowa. One storm produced damaging winds in Taylor County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs down at least 6 inches in diameter.  Big enough to require the use of a tractor/vehicle to move them.  Delayed report, time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped through the area with a large MCS developing across central Iowa throughout the overnight hours and continued into the early morning hours of the 15th. Storms continued to track over the same areas resulting in heavy rainfall across large portions of central Iowa. Flash flooding was reported in several areas of Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet reported heavy rainfall of 2.40 inches reported over the last 24 hours.  Mesonet station 1 WNW Ankeny SAKI4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the state during the day, dragging behind it a cold front that helped trigger off storms across portions of central and southern Iowa. Hail, damaging winds and a tornado occurred with the storms. The tornado moved from Monroe County into Wapello County damaging multiple farmsteads, power poles and a house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chaser reported large trees down across roads just north of Ottumwa.

Resulting rainfall totals exceeded 3 inches in many locations and a few eclipsing 5 and 6 inches.  Widespread flash flooding and eventually river flooding occurred across large portions of the area mentioned above. Precipitable water values were in the 1.5 to 2.2 inch range with the freezing level around 14 to 15 kft across the state throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 1.75 inches of rain in 20 minutes along with 50 mph or greater winds and power out at their home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the state during the day, dragging behind it a cold front that helped trigger off storms across portions of central and southern Iowa. Hail, damaging winds and a tornado occurred with the storms. The tornado moved from Monroe County into Wapello County damaging multiple farmsteads, power poles and a house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado moved from Monroe into Wapello county.  This is the Wapello county portion.  The circulation for this short-lived tornado originated on the northern flank of a small squall line bow segment. This circulation rapidly tightened south of Eddyville and broke off the line, where it became tornadic. Damage included multiple farmsteads that lost silos and outbuildings, bent power lines, snapped trees, and a house that lost its roof and east wall. Downburst damage also occurred upstream and downstream of the tornado track.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough pushed through the northern plains with an associated cold front extending southward into the central plains. Storms fired along the front to the west of Iowa in the evening and developed into a large MCS as it moved into Iowa. An MCV and a large squall line moved through northern and central Iowa during the overnight hours producing strong, damaging winds and heavy rain. Four brief tornadoes also occurred in central Iowa along the leading edge of the squall line in an area of stronger shear and higher moisture. The tornadoes mainly damaged trees and crops, however one did strike a barn and silo in rural Boone County. Another damaged traffic signals and an Iowa DNR nursery in the Ames area of Story County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-four hour rainfall of 2.26 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system lifted northeast out of the southwest U.S. during the early morning hours of the 17th.  The surface low was over South Dakota as a line of thunderstorms developed ahead of an advancing dry line that was south of the low.  The atmosphere was moderately unstable with about 2000 J/kg as the storms developed in the evening.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was only about 400 J/kg with a freezing level around 13,000 feet.  The lifted index was in the -4 to -5 C range as the storms approached with 40 to 45 kts of shear available.  The LCL was quite low, around 750 meters.  CAPE in the zero to 3 km layer was 50 to 100 J/kg.  Precipitable water values were quite high, in the 1.25 to 1.5 inch range.  For the most part, the thunderstorms were not severe and produced mainly heavy rainfall.  Several reports of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall were received from southwest into north central Iowa.  As the line advanced eastward, a small mesoscale vorticity maximum was observed riding north along the line.  The thunderstorms became enhanced as it moved north with a line of damage extending from Clarke County north to Webster County.  Eighty two cars of a railroad train were derailed by the winds, with two of them blown off into the nearby field.  Numerous very large trees were downed and some farm buildings lost roofs.  Reports of trees and power lines being blown down continued north as the vorticity maximum lifted north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage reported with tree tops snapped, a flag pole was bent and heavy outdoor objects were moved.

By the evening vigorous storms fired up just north of the boundary and continued as the low level jet began to pick up.  Numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Fairly quickly after sunset, storms transitioned away from multi-cellular severe to training heavy rain producers over an area NW to SE roughly from Carroll to Bloomfield, including portions of the Des Moines metro.  Resulting rainfall totals exceeded 3 inches in many locations and a few eclipsing 5 and 6 inches.  Widespread flash flooding and eventually river flooding occurred across large portions of the area mentioned above. Precipitable water values were in the 1.5 to 2.2 inch range with the freezing level around 14 to 15 kft across the state throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a lot of nickel sized hail and very heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough pushed through the northern plains with an associated cold front extending southward into the central plains. Storms fired along the front to the west of Iowa in the evening and developed into a large MCS as it moved into Iowa. An MCV and a large squall line moved through northern and central Iowa during the overnight hours producing strong, damaging winds and heavy rain. Four brief tornadoes also occurred in central Iowa along the leading edge of the squall line in an area of stronger shear and higher moisture. The tornadoes mainly damaged trees and crops, however one did strike a barn and silo in rural Boone County. Another damaged traffic signals and an Iowa DNR nursery in the Ames area of Story County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees up to 8 inches in diameter snapped off with larger trees uprooted. Most of the damage concentrated SW of the airport and University Ave. A stop sign was snapped off at that intersection as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A primarily heavy rain event affecting the southern half of Iowa. Storms were ongoing across portions of central Iowa during the day, with an area of low pressure centered across north central Kansas. Additional storm development occurred across southern Iowa to the north of a stationary front draped eastward from the surface low across northern Missouri. A few severe reports occurred with some large hail reported in Taylor County and damaging winds across portions of Taylor, Appanoose and Davis Counties. Flash flooding was also reported with the heavy rainfall in Taylor County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported numerous tree branches down in the West Grove community.  Also reported metal roof damage to a local business.  Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the afternoon storms fired up ahead of and along a cold front that was entering Iowa from the northwest.  Numerous storms had severe hail, produced damaging winds, and two tornadoes. The first tornado was on the ground for several miles tracking through portions of Marion, Lucas and Monroe Counties. The second tornado formed just west of Albia and struck the town of Albia damaging several homes and businesses. Flash flooding was also reported in Davis and Monroe Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line moved across northeast Iowa on June 22nd. This squall line produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and at least one tornado. The tornado destroyed one farm house near Maynard (Fayette County) causing injuries to both occupants of the house. Several other nearby farms sustained damage to crops, trees, and barns.  As the squall line moved across northeast Iowa it produced a swath of wind damage starting near Marble Rock (Floyd County) to around Guttenberg (Clayton County). Within this swath, a 65 mph wind gust was measured in Marble Rock that took down trees and power lines, around 17 farms sustained damage across southern Fayette County, and an Amish grocery store was destroyed near Garber (Clayton County). Preliminary damage estimates for Fayette County are in excess of $3 million. Locally heavy rains also produced some flash flooding with 480th Street washed out south of Stacyville (Floyd County) and Waterloo Creek came out of its banks near Dorchester (Allamakee County) within 30 minutes of the rain starting. The State of Iowa Governor issued a disaster proclamation for the following counties: Allamakee, Clayton, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek. A federal disaster declaration was made for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Clayton, Fayette, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain produced street flooding in McGregor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A primarily heavy rain event affecting the southern half of Iowa. Storms were ongoing across portions of central Iowa during the day, with an area of low pressure centered across north central Kansas. Additional storm development occurred across southern Iowa to the north of a stationary front draped eastward from the surface low across northern Missouri. A few severe reports occurred with some large hail reported in Taylor County and damaging winds across portions of Taylor, Appanoose and Davis Counties. Flash flooding was also reported with the heavy rainfall in Taylor County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 24 hour rainfall total of 2.23 inches measured at KWBG in Boone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across much of central and southern Iowa toward the latter portion of the month leading to flooding along many of the rivers across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Raccoon River at Redfield crested at 22.65 feet at 3:45 PM CST on 25 June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line moved across northeast Iowa on June 22nd. This squall line produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and at least one tornado. The tornado destroyed one farm house near Maynard (Fayette County) causing injuries to both occupants of the house. Several other nearby farms sustained damage to crops, trees, and barns.  As the squall line moved across northeast Iowa it produced a swath of wind damage starting near Marble Rock (Floyd County) to around Guttenberg (Clayton County). Within this swath, a 65 mph wind gust was measured in Marble Rock that took down trees and power lines, around 17 farms sustained damage across southern Fayette County, and an Amish grocery store was destroyed near Garber (Clayton County). Preliminary damage estimates for Fayette County are in excess of $3 million. Locally heavy rains also produced some flash flooding with 480th Street washed out south of Stacyville (Floyd County) and Waterloo Creek came out of its banks near Dorchester (Allamakee County) within 30 minutes of the rain starting. The State of Iowa Governor issued a disaster proclamation for the following counties: Allamakee, Clayton, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek. A federal disaster declaration was made for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Clayton, Fayette, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell in Elkader. Around a 1000 acres of corn and beans were damaged by the hail northeast of Elkader.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak warm front sparked afternoon and evening thunderstorms with locally heavy rains and a couple of isolated flash flooding events in southeastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations in Mount Pleasant were barricaded and closed due to the deep water over them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak warm front sparked afternoon and evening thunderstorms with locally heavy rains and a couple of isolated flash flooding events in southeastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over numerous roads to a depth of over 6 inches and some of the water was flowing in a few locations. At least one vehicle was stalled in the flood water in the middle of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly northward moving boundary/warm front was draped across the area throughout the day.  By the evening vigorous storms fired up just north of the boundary and continued as the low level jet began to pick up.  Numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Fairly quickly after sunset, storms transitioned away from multi-cellular severe to training heavy rain producers over an area NW to SE roughly from Carroll to Bloomfield, including portions of the Des Moines metro.  Resulting rainfall totals exceeded 3 inches in many locations and a few eclipsing 5 and 6 inches.  Widespread flash flooding and eventually river flooding occurred across large portions of the area mentioned above. Precipitable water values were in the 1.5 to 2.2 inch range with the freezing level around 14 to 15 kft across the state throughout the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of funnel cloud reports were associated with a few weak storms that formed  across central Iowa during the late afternoon. Zero to 3 km CAPE was very strong at around 150 to 200 J/kg with some surface vorticity in place. LCLs were low around 750 m and the Non-Supercell Tornado parameter had a 2 around south central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported receiving multiple reports from the public of a funnel cloud dropping from and receding into the cloud base northwest of Oskaloosa over the previous 5 minutes.  Rotation was also noted in clouds southwest of New Sharon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the state during the day, dragging behind it a cold front that helped trigger off storms across portions of central and southern Iowa. Hail, damaging winds and a tornado occurred with the storms. The tornado moved from Monroe County into Wapello County damaging multiple farmsteads, power poles and a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across portions of central and southern Iowa during the last week of July. This led to flooding at a few locations along the Des Moines River and also on Walnut Creek and the Middle River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Eddyville crested a second time in as many days at 62.00 feet at 8:00 AM CDT on 29 July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary was pushing through the state with ongoing convection through central Iowa through the day. As the boundary neared the area, the convection intensified and impacted central and southern Iowa in the evening to early morning hours. Two thunderstorm complexes moved through portions of central and southern Iowa with very heavy rainfall causing flash flooding. The severe thunderstorm complexes produced some hail but mainly strong damaging winds. Precipitable water values were near 2 inches in some areas with deep warm cloud depths during the event. This heavy rainfall further exacerbated conditions as some of the area had just received heavy rainfall in previous days. This led to some extended flooding for a few days and flooding on some area rivers. Several locations reported in excess of 4 inches of rain, while one location in Warren County reported over 7.50 inches of rainfall. The Emergency Manager for Marion County mentioned widespread reports of 8 to 10 inches with one report close to 12 inches of accumulated rainfall over the past few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported heavy rainfall of 4.26 inches over the last 24 hours, since 7 am the previous day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly northward moving boundary/warm front was draped across the area throughout the day.  By the evening vigorous storms fired up just north of the boundary and continued as the low level jet began to pick up.  Numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Fairly quickly after sunset, storms transitioned away from multi-cellular severe to training heavy rain producers over an area NW to SE roughly from Carroll to Bloomfield, including portions of the Des Moines metro.  Resulting rainfall totals exceeded 3 inches in many locations and a few eclipsing 5 and 6 inches.  Widespread flash flooding and eventually river flooding occurred across large portions of the area mentioned above. Precipitable water values were in the 1.5 to 2.2 inch range with the freezing level around 14 to 15 kft across the state throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported heavy rainfall of 1.92 inches of rain over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The evening of the 23rd and into the AM hours of the 24th a few training storms set up over central Iowa, namely Ames area, and produced locally heavy rainfall and a couple of hail reports.  The setup was a warm front/pseudo stationary front in the region and low level jet ramping up.

A cold front passing late in the afternoon spawned scattered thunderstorms that produced a few severe storms and localized heavy rains that resulted in a couple of flash flooding events.

Additionally, a large amount of water crossed 29th Street near Duck Creek Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raccoon River at Fleur Drive Des Moines crested at 12.83 feet at 5:15 PM CDT on 29 July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line moved across northeast Iowa on June 22nd. This squall line produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and at least one tornado. The tornado destroyed one farm house near Maynard (Fayette County) causing injuries to both occupants of the house. Several other nearby farms sustained damage to crops, trees, and barns.  As the squall line moved across northeast Iowa it produced a swath of wind damage starting near Marble Rock (Floyd County) to around Guttenberg (Clayton County). Within this swath, a 65 mph wind gust was measured in Marble Rock that took down trees and power lines, around 17 farms sustained damage across southern Fayette County, and an Amish grocery store was destroyed near Garber (Clayton County). Preliminary damage estimates for Fayette County are in excess of $3 million. Locally heavy rains also produced some flash flooding with 480th Street washed out south of Stacyville (Floyd County) and Waterloo Creek came out of its banks near Dorchester (Allamakee County) within 30 minutes of the rain starting. The State of Iowa Governor issued a disaster proclamation for the following counties: Allamakee, Clayton, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek. A federal disaster declaration was made for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Clayton, Fayette, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large area of wind damage occurred between Guttenberg and Millvile along U.S. Highway 52. A barn roof was blown off and several other barns were blown down near Millville. Numerous trees were blown down causing U.S. Highway 52 to be closed and a parked semi and trailer were overturned by the winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line moved across northeast Iowa on June 22nd. This squall line produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and at least one tornado. The tornado destroyed one farm house near Maynard (Fayette County) causing injuries to both occupants of the house. Several other nearby farms sustained damage to crops, trees, and barns.  As the squall line moved across northeast Iowa it produced a swath of wind damage starting near Marble Rock (Floyd County) to around Guttenberg (Clayton County). Within this swath, a 65 mph wind gust was measured in Marble Rock that took down trees and power lines, around 17 farms sustained damage across southern Fayette County, and an Amish grocery store was destroyed near Garber (Clayton County). Preliminary damage estimates for Fayette County are in excess of $3 million. Locally heavy rains also produced some flash flooding with 480th Street washed out south of Stacyville (Floyd County) and Waterloo Creek came out of its banks near Dorchester (Allamakee County) within 30 minutes of the rain starting. The State of Iowa Governor issued a disaster proclamation for the following counties: Allamakee, Clayton, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek. A federal disaster declaration was made for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Clayton, Fayette, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fields of corn were flattened and large tree branches were blow down west of Nashua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave on a northward moving warm front produced scattered thunderstorms along the boundary.  Low level wind shear was favorable for severe weather and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado with maximum winds estimated at 80 mph touched down 1.9 miles southwest of Swedesburg, IA.  It traveled 1.51 miles and lifted 0.8 miles south of Swedesburg.  The average path width was 10 yards.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary was pushing through the state with ongoing convection through central Iowa through the day. As the boundary neared the area, the convection intensified and impacted central and southern Iowa in the evening to early morning hours. Two thunderstorm complexes moved through portions of central and southern Iowa with very heavy rainfall causing flash flooding. The severe thunderstorm complexes produced some hail but mainly strong damaging winds. Precipitable water values were near 2 inches in some areas with deep warm cloud depths during the event. This heavy rainfall further exacerbated conditions as some of the area had just received heavy rainfall in previous days. This led to some extended flooding for a few days and flooding on some area rivers. Several locations reported in excess of 4 inches of rain, while one location in Warren County reported over 7.50 inches of rainfall. The Emergency Manager for Marion County mentioned widespread reports of 8 to 10 inches with one report close to 12 inches of accumulated rainfall over the past few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported several roads in Corning and Prescott have street flooding.  Also reported several gravel roads south of Highway 34 are flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line moved across northeast Iowa on June 22nd. This squall line produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and at least one tornado. The tornado destroyed one farm house near Maynard (Fayette County) causing injuries to both occupants of the house. Several other nearby farms sustained damage to crops, trees, and barns.  As the squall line moved across northeast Iowa it produced a swath of wind damage starting near Marble Rock (Floyd County) to around Guttenberg (Clayton County). Within this swath, a 65 mph wind gust was measured in Marble Rock that took down trees and power lines, around 17 farms sustained damage across southern Fayette County, and an Amish grocery store was destroyed near Garber (Clayton County). Preliminary damage estimates for Fayette County are in excess of $3 million. Locally heavy rains also produced some flash flooding with 480th Street washed out south of Stacyville (Floyd County) and Waterloo Creek came out of its banks near Dorchester (Allamakee County) within 30 minutes of the rain starting. The State of Iowa Governor issued a disaster proclamation for the following counties: Allamakee, Clayton, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek. A federal disaster declaration was made for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Clayton, Fayette, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two small creeks north of McIntire went out of their banks and sent water over Walnut Avenue in the vicinity of 500th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary was pushing through the state with ongoing convection through central Iowa through the day. As the boundary neared the area, the convection intensified and impacted central and southern Iowa in the evening to early morning hours. Two thunderstorm complexes moved through portions of central and southern Iowa with very heavy rainfall causing flash flooding. The severe thunderstorm complexes produced some hail but mainly strong damaging winds. Precipitable water values were near 2 inches in some areas with deep warm cloud depths during the event. This heavy rainfall further exacerbated conditions as some of the area had just received heavy rainfall in previous days. This led to some extended flooding for a few days and flooding on some area rivers. Several locations reported in excess of 4 inches of rain, while one location in Warren County reported over 7.50 inches of rainfall. The Emergency Manager for Marion County mentioned widespread reports of 8 to 10 inches with one report close to 12 inches of accumulated rainfall over the past few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown over in town. Sweet corn was also blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS moved into the state in the early morning hours from Minnesota, producing heavy rainfall across large portions of north-central Iowa.  Highest amounts reported were generally in the 4-4.50 range with a 4.60 report in Winnebago county.  The MCS progressed generally S and SSE across central Iowa through the morning hours and was able to strengthen enough by the early afternoon to produce wind damage reports in far south central Iowa, including a few large trees, overturned campers, and downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall from morning thunderstorms of 4.60 inches of rain.  Ending at 8:45am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved slowly southeast across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the day on July 24th. Ahead of the front, isolated thunderstorms developed during the early morning on the 24th as moisture returned to the region. Later in the day, extreme instability developed ahead of the front as temperatures soared into the upper 90s coupled with dew points in the mid and upper 70s. Two rounds of thunderstorms developed in association with the front. The first was in the late afternoon over east central Nebraska into southwest Iowa, and then a second round occurred as storms moved into the region from central Nebraska around midnight crossing into southwest Iowa early in the morning on the 25th. Both of these rounds produced isolated severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated wind gusts to 60 mph.

A volatile atmosphere developed over western Iowa during the afternoon of June 24th with the juxtaposition of high instability, strong wind shear, and a warm front in the area. A strong capping inversion prevented widespread severe weather, but one isolated supercell developed in Shelby County and tracked southeast. The storm produced some hail and several reports of wall clouds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS moved into the state in the early morning hours from Minnesota, producing heavy rainfall across large portions of north-central Iowa.  Highest amounts reported were generally in the 4-4.50 range with a 4.60 report in Winnebago county.  The MCS progressed generally S and SSE across central Iowa through the morning hours and was able to strengthen enough by the early afternoon to produce wind damage reports in far south central Iowa, including a few large trees, overturned campers, and downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall from thunderstorms earlier today of 2.70 inches.

The front progressed slowly, aiding in keeping the rain over the same areas for long periods of time.  By the end of the day, a number of areas across the south reported 4+ inches of rain with the highest totals around 5.5-5.75 near Lorimor, Rathbun, Derby, and Humeston.  Localized flash flooding ensued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell north of Interstate 80 in Iowa and Illinois, totaling 1 to 2 inches. Early in the evening, an arctic cold front swept through from west to east, producing strong winds and a short period of convective snow showers. This combined to produce a period of blowing snow with visibility well under a mile, and near zero for brief times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing snow reduced visibility to under 1 mile, and near zero at times with brief snow showers and strong winds gusts to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure, associated with record or near record surface pressure, settled into the mid Missouri River Valley during the early morning on the 7th. The combination of arctic air with actual air temperatures from 5 below to 5 above zero, and winds of 15 to 30 mph with gusts to near 40 mph created wind chill values of 15 to 25 below zero starting in the early morning on the 7th continuing through the early afternoon in many areas.

Early in the evening, an arctic cold front swept through from west to east, producing strong winds and a short period of convective snow showers. This combined to produce a period of blowing snow with visibility well under a mile, and near zero for brief times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing snow reduced visibility to under 1 mile, and near zero at times with brief snow showers and strong winds gusts near 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS moved into the state in the early morning hours from Minnesota, producing heavy rainfall across large portions of north-central Iowa.  Highest amounts reported were generally in the 4-4.50 range with a 4.60 report in Winnebago county.  The MCS progressed generally S and SSE across central Iowa through the morning hours and was able to strengthen enough by the early afternoon to produce wind damage reports in far south central Iowa, including a few large trees, overturned campers, and downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mason City Municipal Airport ASOS reported heavy rainfall of 4.13 inches since midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow spread across the Midwest the afternoon of January 5, 2015, with accumulations in Eastern Iowa beginning mid afternoon, and spreading into Northern Illinois mid evening. Widespread 4 to 7 inch amounts were reported, with numerous school cancellations on January 6th. Due to the cold air in place, the snow was fluffy in nature, but with little wind, blowing snow was not an issue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated up to 7 inches in northwest Iowa from late morning to the evening of January 5th. The snow was accompanied by southeast winds averaging 15 to 30 mph, with some higher gusts, causing areas of blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 3 to 6 inches, including 4 inches at Lester. The snow was accompanied by southeast winds gusting to over 30 mph, reducing visibility to a quarter mile or less in a few areas. The storm forced some schools to close early for the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 7 inches in the Spirit Lake to Storm Lake areas of northwest Iowa from early morning to early afternoon of February 25th. Lesser snow accumulations were reported further west in northwest Iowa. The snow was accompanied by northeast winds gusting to 35 mph over the warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 7 inches, including 6.5 inches at Spirit Lake. The snow was accompanied by northeast winds gusting to 30 mph which caused areas of blowing snow, reducing visibility to 1/4 mile at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving storm system occurred from the mid-morning on Feb 25th to the early morning on Feb 26th. An area of low pressure moved across the Plains and into Missouri spreading widespread snow across the region. Widespread snowfall totals between 3 and 6 inches were common.  The heaviest snowfall of 6 to 7.5 inches generally fell along and southwest of a line from Cedar Rapids, to Muscatine, to Macomb, IL.  Northerly winds of 10 to 20 mph resulted in some snow drifts of 1 to 2 feet in parts of southeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported 5.3 of snow in Fairfield.  Other reports were between 3 and 5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough across the southwest United States moved into the southern Plains early on January 31st. Ahead of this system deep moisture spread northward across the central Plains. This broad, but persistent area of warm air advection led to increasing showers over Kansas, which moved northward into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa early on the 31st. This initial precipitation was a mix of light rain and snow through mid afternoon. As colder air, associated with a stronger northern stream trough and cold front, started to move into the area late Saturday afternoon and evening the precipitation turned to all snow and began to accumulate. Snow, occasionally moderate, continued into Saturday night diminishing to light snow on Sunday morning, before ending Sunday afternoon. Snowfall of 5 to 9 inches were common across east central and southeast Nebraska, as well as southwest Iowa. As the colder air moved into the area Saturday night winds switched from northeast to northwest and increased. Winds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts over 40 mph were common into the day on Sunday that led to considerable blowing and drifting snow. The combination of falling and blowing snow resulted in reduced visibilities and very difficult driving conditions. This winter storm continued into February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 7 to 9 inches across the county, including a measured 8.5 inches in Red Oak and 9 inches in Emerson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused damaging winds at several locations in northwest Iowa from just before to a little after sunrise on the morning of June 22nd. There was also one report of penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including a 14 inch diameter tree snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into eastern Nebraska during the afternoon of the 1st. Ahead of the cold front some moisture return from the southern Pains coupled with steep lapse-rates aloft to create marginal instability. Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed along the cold front by late afternoon, developing into a line of thunderstorms before moving southeast through eastern Nebraska and into western Iowa. Isolated severe weather was reported along this line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer reported winds in excess of 60 mph.

An area of low pressure moved southeast across the Mississippi River Valley on February 25th. This system produced a band of snow from eastern South Dakota across southern Minnesota and northern Iowa into northern Illinois. Most of the accumulations in this band ranged from 2 to 5 inches. However, over portions of Fayette and Clayton Counties, the snowfall increased with amounts of 5 to 7 inches common. The highest reported total was 7.4 inches near Volga (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 5 to 6 inches of snow across Fayette County. The highest reported total was 6.2 inches in the city of Fayette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northeast Iowa experienced blizzard conditions from the afternoon of January 8th into the very early morning hours of the 9th. Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph, with gusts as high as 50 mph, produced widespread whiteout conditions where the visibility was reduced to a quarter mile or less. The winds also caused severe drifting to occur, making many roads impassable and the Iowa Department of Transportation pulled the plows off the roads early in the evening. The whiteout conditions started occurring during the afternoon of the 8th ahead of an area of low pressure and cold front. Light snow fell ahead of the cold front, with 1 to 2 inches of accumulations, and was accompanied by south winds gusting to 25 to 30 mph. Once the cold front passed through, the winds switched to the northwest and increased to produce widespread whiteout conditions from the light snow that had fallen earlier in the day. These conditions then continued well past nightfall before the winds diminished during the early morning hours of the 9th. Most schools across the area dismissed early on the 8th and also had delayed starts on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained south winds of 15 to 25 mph, with higher gusts, produced sporadic whiteouts during the early afternoon of January 8th. Conditions deteriorated once a cold front moved through. The winds became northwest at 20 to 35 mph, with higher gusts, which produced widespread whiteouts from the 1 to 2 inches of snow that had fallen earlier in the day. The blowing and drifting snow created very poor driving conditions and most of the schools in Fayette County dismissed early on the 8th and all of them started late on the 9th.

Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph, with gusts as high as 50 mph, produced widespread whiteout conditions where the visibility was reduced to a quarter mile or less. The winds also caused severe drifting to occur, making many roads impassable and the Iowa Department of Transportation pulled the plows off the roads early in the evening. The whiteout conditions started occurring during the afternoon of the 8th ahead of an area of low pressure and cold front. Light snow fell ahead of the cold front, with 1 to 2 inches of accumulations, and was accompanied by south winds gusting to 25 to 30 mph. Once the cold front passed through, the winds switched to the northwest and increased to produce widespread whiteout conditions from the light snow that had fallen earlier in the day. These conditions then continued well past nightfall before the winds diminished during the early morning hours of the 9th. Most schools across the area dismissed early on the 8th and also had delayed starts on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained south winds of 15 to 25 mph, with higher gusts, produced sporadic whiteouts during the early afternoon of January 8th. Conditions deteriorated once a cold front moved through. The winds became northwest at 20 to 35 mph, with higher gusts, which produced widespread whiteouts from the 1 to 2 inches of snow that had fallen earlier in the day. The blowing and drifting snow created very poor driving conditions and most of the schools in Howard County dismissed early on the 8th and all of them started late on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged snow event occurred from the mid-afternoon on Jan 31st to the late evening on Feb 1st.  A strong area of low pressure moved across Missouri and southern Illinois spreading widespread snow across the region.  The heaviest snowfall of 9 to 15 inches generally fell along the Interstate 80 corridor.  Gusty northwest winds developed behind the system, resulting in considerable blowing and drifting snow on Feb 1st.  Several areas experienced prolonged power outages and downed tree limbs due to the heavy wet snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer measured 9 inches of snow in Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain during the early and mid morning of January 19th caused icy roads and walking surfaces in part of northwest Iowa. Ice accumulations were light.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain coated roads and other surfaces with a thin layer of ice which lasted until the temperature rose above freezing in the late morning. Several vehicles slid off roads and into ditches, including one which rolled on its side northwest of Le Mars, requiring extrication of the driver. There were no reported injuries.

The snow and blowing snow began late on January 31st and lasted through much of February 1st. The main impact of the storm was to cause difficulties in weekend travel for those who did not delay or change their travel plans.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of northeast Iowa received heavy snow on the last day of January. The area was set up to for heavy snow after a cold front moved through the region on the 30th. This front was not very far south of the region as an area of low pressure moved along it, throwing moisture from the Gulf of Mexico into the cold air. As a result, heavy snow started falling during the evening of the 31st. This heavy snow continued into February 1st with two day totals of 5 to 9 inches common. The highest reported total was 11 inches in Oelwein (Fayette County). In addition to the snow, the area of low pressure also produced winds of 20 to 40 mph that caused considerable drifting to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported heavy snow across Allamakee County during the evening of January 31st. The heavy snow continued into February 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving storm system occurred from the mid-morning on Feb 25th to the early morning on Feb 26th. An area of low pressure moved across the Plains and into Missouri spreading widespread snow across the region. Widespread snowfall totals between 3 and 6 inches were common.  The heaviest snowfall of 6 to 7.5 inches generally fell along and southwest of a line from Cedar Rapids, to Muscatine, to Macomb, IL.  Northerly winds of 10 to 20 mph resulted in some snow drifts of 1 to 2 feet in parts of southeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm spotter reported 6.7 snow in Independence.  Other reports within the county were between 4 to 6 inches of snow.

Low pressure passed to the north of Iowa.  A trough of low pressure extended south from the low and swept across the state.  The airmass was cold with Arctic air in place.  Recent snowfall remained loose on the ground across the area with several inches of relatively fluffy snow present.  As the trough moved through the state, snow squalls occurred with it.  This made a potentially dangerous situation more likely.  The winds increased during the day with wind speeds during the afternoon and evening hours in the 25 to 40 MPH range with frequent gusts to around 50 MPH.  The combination of snow on the ground and the snow squalls resulted in rapid visibility reductions.  The snow squalls generally produced under an inch of new snowfall.  By mid afternoon, whiteout conditions were widespread across north central Iowa.  Travel was hazardous with many roads closed or nearly impassible.  One fatality occurred.  A couple, 76 and 77 years of age, drove off the road in Hancock County, near Woden, and sought help on foot.  The 76 year old female was found frozen to death, the husband was later found and taken to the hospital in critical condition suffering from exposure.

A deep trough across the southwest United States moved into the southern Plains early on January 31st. Ahead of this system deep moisture spread northward across the central Plains. This broad, but persistent area of warm air advection led to increasing showers over Kansas, which moved northward into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa early on the 31st. This initial precipitation was a mix of light rain and snow through mid afternoon. As colder air, associated with a stronger northern stream trough and cold front, started to move into the area late Saturday afternoon and evening the precipitation turned to all snow and began to accumulate. Snow, occasionally moderate, continued into Saturday night diminishing to light snow on Sunday morning, before ending Sunday afternoon. Snowfall of 5 to 9 inches were common across east central and southeast Nebraska, as well as southwest Iowa. As the colder air moved into the area Saturday night winds switched from northeast to northwest and increased. Winds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts over 40 mph were common into the day on Sunday that led to considerable blowing and drifting snow. The combination of falling and blowing snow resulted in reduced visibilities and very difficult driving conditions. This winter storm continued into February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 6 to 8 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged snow event occurred from the mid-afternoon on Jan 31st to the late evening on Feb 1st.  A strong area of low pressure moved across Missouri and southern Illinois spreading widespread snow across the region.  The heaviest snowfall of 9 to 15 inches generally fell along the Interstate 80 corridor.  Gusty northwest winds developed behind the system, resulting in considerable blowing and drifting snow on Feb 1st.  Several areas experienced prolonged power outages and downed tree limbs due to the heavy wet snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported 11.3 inches of snow in Manchester.

A storm system moved southeast from the Dakotas into the central Mississippi River Valley from March 22nd into the 23rd. Heavy snow fell north of this system, with parts of northeast Iowa receiving between 6 and 12 inches. The snow fell at rates of 2 inches an hour at times, resulting in quick accumulations. Several accidents occurred as the roads quickly became snow covered. Where the heavy snow fell, almost all the schools were closed on the 23rd and those that were not, had delayed starts. The highest reported snowfall total was 12 inches in Waukon (Allamakee County) and Marquette (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 2 to 12 inches of snow across Clayton County. The heaviest snow of 6 inches or greater fell across the northern half of the county. The highest reported total was 12 inches in Marquette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow spread across the Midwest the afternoon of January 5, 2015, with accumulations in Eastern Iowa beginning mid afternoon, and spreading into Northern Illinois mid evening. Widespread 4 to 7 inch amounts were reported, with numerous school cancellations on January 6th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough across the southwest United States moved into the southern Plains early on January 31st. Ahead of this system deep moisture spread northward across the central Plains. This broad, but persistent area of warm air advection led to increasing showers over Kansas, which moved northward into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa early on the 31st. This initial precipitation was a mix of light rain and snow through mid afternoon. As colder air, associated with a stronger northern stream trough and cold front, started to move into the area late Saturday afternoon and evening the precipitation turned to all snow and began to accumulate. Snow, occasionally moderate, continued into Saturday night diminishing to light snow on Sunday morning, before ending Sunday afternoon. Snowfall of 5 to 9 inches were common across east central and southeast Nebraska, as well as southwest Iowa. As the colder air moved into the area Saturday night winds switched from northeast to northwest and increased. Winds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts over 40 mph were common into the day on Sunday that led to considerable blowing and drifting snow. The combination of falling and blowing snow resulted in reduced visibilities and very difficult driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 6 to 8 inches across the county including a measured 8 inches in Clarinda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed to the north of Iowa.  A trough of low pressure extended south from the low and swept across the state.  The airmass was cold with Arctic air in place.  Recent snowfall remained loose on the ground across the area with several inches of relatively fluffy snow present.  As the trough moved through the state, snow squalls occurred with it.  This made a potentially dangerous situation more likely.  The winds increased during the day with wind speeds during the afternoon and evening hours in the 25 to 40 MPH range with frequent gusts to around 50 MPH.  The combination of snow on the ground and the snow squalls resulted in rapid visibility reductions.  The snow squalls generally produced under an inch of new snowfall.  By mid afternoon, whiteout conditions were widespread across north central Iowa.  Travel was hazardous with many roads closed or nearly impassible.  One fatality occurred.  A couple, 76 and 77 years of age, drove off the road in Hancock County, near Woden, and sought help on foot.  The 76 year old female was found frozen to death, the husband was later found and taken to the hospital in critical condition suffering from exposure.

A storm system moved southeast from the Dakotas into the central Mississippi River Valley from March 22nd into the 23rd. Heavy snow fell north of this system, with parts of northeast Iowa receiving between 6 and 12 inches. The snow fell at rates of 2 inches an hour at times, resulting in quick accumulations. Several accidents occurred as the roads quickly became snow covered. Where the heavy snow fell, almost all the schools were closed on the 23rd and those that were not, had delayed starts. The highest reported snowfall total was 12 inches in Waukon (Allamakee County) and Marquette (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 8 to 12 inches of snow across Allamakee County. The highest reported total was 12 inches in Waukon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary boundary extended from the Texas Panhandle, across northern Missouri, into southeast Lower Michigan.  Warm air advection continued with an area of elevated thunderstorms developing during the morning hours.  The storms were pulse type severe with the severe threat being hail.  The most unstable CAPE was in the 2000 to 3000 J/kg range, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 700 J/kg.  The atmosphere was moderately sheared with 40 to 45 kts of effective shear available.  One of the storms became severe in the Corning area of Adams County.  The storm produced golf ball size hail.  The storms continued to move east and later produced hail about 1.25 inches in diameter in the Osceola area of Clarke County. As the storms continued to move east, hail around quarter size continues to fall.  The south side of Des Moines received enough hail to completely cover the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved east over Iowa and Illinois which brought widespread thunderstorms with heavy rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported several roads flooded in Tipton, resulting in being barricaded and closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front was sweeping through the state as an upper level wave moved through the region. The front was located stretching across portions of northwest and west central Iowa by late afternoon into the early evening hours. Scattered showers and thunderstorms had developed along and ahead of the frontal boundary throughout the late afternoon hours. One of the storms produced a funnel cloud in the Lytton area of Sac County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief funnel cloud came down and then retreated upwards. The funnel cloud was observed by the Calhoun County Sheriff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed to the north of Iowa.  A trough of low pressure extended south from the low and swept across the state.  The airmass was cold with Arctic air in place.  Recent snowfall remained loose on the ground across the area with several inches of relatively fluffy snow present.  As the trough moved through the state, snow squalls occurred with it.  This made a potentially dangerous situation more likely.  The winds increased during the day with wind speeds during the afternoon and evening hours in the 25 to 40 MPH range with frequent gusts to around 50 MPH.  The combination of snow on the ground and the snow squalls resulted in rapid visibility reductions.  The snow squalls generally produced under an inch of new snowfall.  By mid afternoon, whiteout conditions were widespread across north central Iowa.  Travel was hazardous with many roads closed or nearly impassible.  One fatality occurred.  A couple, 76 and 77 years of age, drove off the road in Hancock County, near Woden, and sought help on foot.  The 76 year old female was found frozen to death, the husband was later found and taken to the hospital in critical condition suffering from exposure.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved across parts of the region Thursday afternoon and evening, as a cold front moved into Iowa.  One thunderstorm was severe, and produced damaging winds and large hail up to the size of quarters.  Another thunderstorm produced penny to nickel sized hail.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved across parts of the region Thursday afternoon and evening, as a cold front moved into Iowa.  One thunderstorm was severe, and produced damaging winds and large hail up to the size of quarters.  Another thunderstorm produced penny to nickel sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported pea to dime sized hail, also strong and gusty winds and heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary boundary extended from the Texas Panhandle, across northern Missouri, into southeast Lower Michigan.  Warm air advection continued with an area of elevated thunderstorms developing during the morning hours.  The storms were pulse type severe with the severe threat being hail.  The most unstable CAPE was in the 2000 to 3000 J/kg range, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 700 J/kg.  The atmosphere was moderately sheared with 40 to 45 kts of effective shear available.  One of the storms became severe in the Corning area of Adams County.  The storm produced golf ball size hail.  The storms continued to move east and later produced hail about 1.25 inches in diameter in the Osceola area of Clarke County. As the storms continued to move east, hail around quarter size continues to fall.  The south side of Des Moines received enough hail to completely cover the ground.

The overall parameters remained little changed during the day into the evening.  Most unstable CAPE remained in the 1000 to 2000 J/kg range with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 600 J/kg.  The atmosphere was moderately sheared with 45 kts of effective shear available.  A warm front extended east to west along the southern Iowa border.  During the evening, the low level jet increased to around 35 kts over the top of the frontal boundary.  A band of elevated thunderstorms formed north of the front across central Iowa.  The storms were just below severe levels, producing penny to nickel size hail from Guthrie County toward Marshall County.
