A cold front associated with the storm system that brought the heavy rainfall to the area on the evening of the 6th, moved through southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa on the afternoon of the 7th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along the cold front by mid afternoon and continued into the early early evening hours. Isolated storms produced marginal severe weather in both southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.

A closed upper low lifted out to the west of Iowa with the surface low tracking northeast to near the Iowa-South Dakota border.  A warm front extended southeast into Iowa and lifted north as the dry line pushed into western Iowa during the afternoon.  The synoptic situation was fairly classic.  The atmosphere was relatively unstable, though cloudiness limited that somewhat.  CAPE values rose to 1500 to 2000 J/kg with a lifted index around -7 at the storms initiation.  Downdraft CAPE was only 300 J/kg.  There was 45 to 50 kts of effective shear however.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer was around 500 J/kg, with a freezing level around 12,000 feet.  The parameters were favorable for potential tornado development with the LCL only 750 meters and 50 to 100 J/kg of CAPE in the zero to 3 km layer with steep lapse rates.  Thunderstorms formed in an arc across western Iowa and quickly produced gusty winds and small hail.  The cells tracked north as the line lifted northeast. Numerous reports of tornadoes were received.  The reports were from the same tornado.  Debris was reported with the tornado in Carroll County.  Damage as done in the Lake City area of Calhoun County by a tornado, reported to be 1/4 mile wide by then.  The tornado touched down northwest of Lidderdale and tracked northeast, striking the west side of Lake City. The tornado continued passing through rural Calhoun County and hit some power poles on the west side of Rockwell City before lifting about 2 miles north of Rockwell City in rural Calhoun County. Much of the damage included leaning power poles, tree damage and uprooted trees. In the town of Lake City, the roofing material was removed from the High School, and the roof was removed from a double-wide manufactured home and a warehouse building north of town. The tornado was of EF1 strength with winds of 100 MPH.  The track was 23.5 miles long.  Additional thunderstorms produced hail and high winds.  An 80 MPH wind gust was reported west of Glidden, a machine shed was destroyed in Audubon County, and a weak shed was blown over in Cass County.  Winds were measured at 65 MPH in Pocahontas.  A strong storm moved through the Lake City area following the tornado and produced baseball size hail and destroyed a farm outbuilding west of Lidderdale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and poles were down just south of Van Meter near 360th and R Ave.

Surface low pressure tracked east across southern Iowa during the afternoon of the 9th.  A line of thunderstorms formed from the low south along its cold front.  The atmosphere was unstable as temperatures rose into the 70s with dewpoint temperatures in the mid 60s. CAPE had increased to 2000 J/kg by early afternoon with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 500 J/kg.  The LCL was only around 750 meters in a shear environment with 50 kts of effective shear.  The freezing level was around 12,500 feet with a surface based lifted index of -7 C.    Thunderstorms formed in south central Iowa.  They produced hail up to half dollar size as they moved through Decatur, Wayne, and Lucas Counties.  In Lucas County, hail covered the ground and stripped decorative concrete from a retaining wall.  As the line of storms moved through the Albia area of Monroe County, a large tree was downed at the intersection of Highway 137 and Highway 5 caused by high winds.  Scattered damage was reported in the areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level storm system moving through the central Plains brought another round of isolated severe weather to the area. Thunderstorms increased during the early afternoon along and ahead of a Pacific cold front over eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa and moved almost due north. Instability was very limited across the area, but strong shear allowed for a few of the storms to produced severe weather. Most of the severe weather was in the form of strong thunderstorm winds, but some hail was also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across northeast Iowa during the afternoon of May 7th. As these storms moved across the area, they produced hail and straight line damaging winds. The largest hail reported was nickel sized in St. Ansgar (Mitchell County), West Union and Fayette (Fayette County). A barn near West Union was damaged by the straight line winds and in St. Ansgar a large branch was blown down onto a home.

A storm system moved southeast from the Dakotas into the central Mississippi River Valley from March 22nd into the 23rd. Heavy snow fell north of this system, with parts of northeast Iowa receiving between 6 and 12 inches. The snow fell at rates of 2 inches an hour at times, resulting in quick accumulations. Several accidents occurred as the roads quickly became snow covered. Where the heavy snow fell, almost all the schools were closed on the 23rd and those that were not, had delayed starts. The highest reported snowfall total was 12 inches in Waukon (Allamakee County) and Marquette (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 9 to 11 inches of snow across Winneshiek County. The highest reported total was 11.5 inches northeast of Decorah.

A strong upper level trough lifted across Iowa during the afternoon.  Cold air was in place aloft with the freezing level down to around 9,000 feet.  CAPE was limited with only around 500 J/kg available.  Shear and CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was virtually nil.  During the peak afternoon heating, a line of thunderstorms formed as a cold front moved through the state.  Many of the storms produced hail, but much of the hail was small though covering the ground in places.  A stronger storm moved through the Pella area of Marion County with nickel size hail reported at the Pella Airport.  One of the storms became more intense over Marshall County and produced quarter size hail, covering the ground.

A large upper level low pressure area was located over eastern Colorado during the afternoon of the 18th.  Moisture streamed north ahead of it with marginal instability over the state.  CAPE rose to around 1000 J/kg by mid afternoon.  The non-severe tornado index was elevated from southeast into parts of central and north central Iowa.  The freezing level was quite low, around 10,000 feet, however the CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was only around 200 J/kg.  Thunderstorms formed in the afternoon and though most remained below severe levels, there were a few severe reports.  One storm produced quarter size hail that covered the ground in Decatur County.  Another storm produced an apparent microburst that destroyed a machine shed and blew it 600 feet into a field in Marshall County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A machine shed was destroyed and debris was blown 600 feet to the northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold frontal boundary moved into Iowa from the northwest.  The atmosphere was not overly favorable for severe weather.  CAPE was between 1000 and 2000 J/kg, but the atmosphere was weakly sheared with 20 to 25 kts of effective shear.  Little downdraft CAPE was available.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was 400 to 500 J/kg with the freezing level around 11,500 feet.  Thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front.  A few of them produced hail and gusty winds.  A 60 MPH wind gust was reported south of the Mason City area in Cerro Gordo County.  Nickel size hail fell in Franklin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car driver had to pull over due to the strong winds.  Dime size hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same system that caused the severe weather the night before continued to move northeast.  A cold front from the low in northwest Minnesota moved into the state.  Thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front.  Most of the stronger convection was to the north, however the southern extent of the storms reached into Worth County and produced a 63 MPH wind in Grafton. Strong and gusty winds were also reported across much of the area with a tightened pressure gradient in the area and strong winds mixing down from aloft. One of the stronger gusts blew a tree down in Carroll County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor tree branch damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few isolated storms developed along a boundary stretching across west central Iowa into northeastern Iowa, with an upper level low spinning just to the west across the high plains. Some of the stronger storms produced hail in the Callendar area of Webster County where they received two rounds of hail. Dime to penny hail was reported with the first round of hail, with nickel to quarter hail covering the ground with the second round.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported dime to penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough lifted across Iowa during the afternoon.  Cold air was in place aloft with the freezing level down to around 9,000 feet.  CAPE was limited with only around 500 J/kg available.  Shear and CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was virtually nil.  During the peak afternoon heating, a line of thunderstorms formed as a cold front moved through the state.  Many of the storms produced hail, but much of the hail was small though covering the ground in places.  A stronger storm moved through the Pella area of Marion County with nickel size hail reported at the Pella Airport.  One of the storms became more intense over Marshall County and produced quarter size hail, covering the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 30 was covered in quarter size hail and ditches along the highway were full of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped southeast into Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of the 3rd.  The atmosphere became quite unstable with temperatures rising into the low to mid 80s and dewpoints in the 55 to 60 degree range.  As thunderstorms began to form along the front, CAPE had risen to around 2000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 300 and 500 J/kg. The freezing level was between 11,500 and 12,000 feet.  Downdraft CAPE was in the 1000 to 1200 J/kg range.  Though many parameters were favorable, the shear was limited with 25 to 30 kts of effective shear available.  Thunderstorms formed along the cold front and became severe relatively quickly.  The main severe weather with the storms was hail with most of the hail reported around quarter size.  Hail covered the ground in a few areas.  Nearly baseball size hail fell in Calhoun County at Manson.  The storm in Algona produced a 59 MPH wind gust at the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that quarter size hail nearly covered the gruond.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported nickel to quarter size hail in the second round of hail. The hail was covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The overall parameters remained little changed during the day into the evening.  Most unstable CAPE remained in the 1000 to 2000 J/kg range with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 600 J/kg.  The atmosphere was moderately sheared with 45 kts of effective shear available.  A warm front extended east to west along the southern Iowa border.  During the evening, the low level jet increased to around 35 kts over the top of the frontal boundary.  A band of elevated thunderstorms formed north of the front across central Iowa.  The storms were just below severe levels, producing penny to nickel size hail from Guthrie County toward Marshall County.

A quasi-stationary boundary extended from the Texas Panhandle, across northern Missouri, into southeast Lower Michigan.  Warm air advection continued with an area of elevated thunderstorms developing during the morning hours.  The storms were pulse type severe with the severe threat being hail.  The most unstable CAPE was in the 2000 to 3000 J/kg range, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 700 J/kg.  The atmosphere was moderately sheared with 40 to 45 kts of effective shear available.  One of the storms became severe in the Corning area of Adams County.  The storm produced golf ball size hail.  The storms continued to move east and later produced hail about 1.25 inches in diameter in the Osceola area of Clarke County. As the storms continued to move east, hail around quarter size continues to fall.  The south side of Des Moines received enough hail to completely cover the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to golf ball size hail falling in Corning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of northeast Iowa received heavy snow on the first day of February. The area was primed for heavy snow after a cold front moved through the region on January 30th.  Heavy snow started falling during the evening of January 31st and continued into February 1st with two day totals of 5 to 9 inches.  The highest reported total was 11 inches in Oelwein (Fayette County). In addition to snow, winds also gusted to 20 to 40 mph which led to considerable drifting in open country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 4 to 6 inches of snow across Allamakee County from January 31st into February 1st. North winds of 20 to 30 mph produced considerable drifting across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed to the north of Iowa.  A trough of low pressure extended south from the low and swept across the state.  The airmass was cold with Arctic air in place.  Recent snowfall remained loose on the ground across the area with several inches of relatively fluffy snow present.  As the trough moved through the state, snow squalls occurred with it.  This made a potentially dangerous situation more likely.  The winds increased during the day with wind speeds during the afternoon and evening hours in the 25 to 40 MPH range with frequent gusts to around 50 MPH.  The combination of snow on the ground and the snow squalls resulted in rapid visibility reductions.  The snow squalls generally produced under an inch of new snowfall.  By mid afternoon, whiteout conditions were widespread across north central Iowa.  Travel was hazardous with many roads closed or nearly impassible.  One fatality occurred.  A couple, 76 and 77 years of age, drove off the road in Hancock County, near Woden, and sought help on foot.  The 76 year old female was found frozen to death, the husband was later found and taken to the hospital in critical condition suffering from exposure.

An active early June weather pattern continued into the day on June 6th. Warm front lifted northward from Kansas and Missouri into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. This allowed for moisture to return to the region and instability to increase. At the same time a storm system moving across the northern Plains allowed for a cold front to move into the region. Thunderstorms developed by mid to to late afternoon at the intersection of where these fronts met. As these thunderstorms moved east and southeast across the area during the evening isolated severe hail and strong were reported with this line. There were also periods of heavy rainfall observed in northeast Nebraska as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer reported winds to near 60 mph. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With ground already saturated from repeated heavy rainfall events in May and early June, another complex of thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall moved across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa starting during the evening on the 10th and continued into the early morning hours on the 11th. This was followed with additional thunderstorms on the afternoon of the 11th over southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. This additional rainfall led to more significant flooding along area rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was observed along the main stem of the Nishnabotna River near Hamburg.  The flooding was confined to agricultural low lands within the levee.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was observed along the East Nishnabotna River near Red Oak. The river crested 2.23 feet above flood stage at 20.23 feet. This resulted in flooding generally of agricultural low lands along the west bank of the river.

A closed upper low lifted out to the west of Iowa with the surface low tracking northeast to near the Iowa-South Dakota border.  A warm front extended southeast into Iowa and lifted north as the dry line pushed into western Iowa during the afternoon.  The synoptic situation was fairly classic.  The atmosphere was relatively unstable, though cloudiness limited that somewhat.  CAPE values rose to 1500 to 2000 J/kg with a lifted index around -7 at the storms initiation.  Downdraft CAPE was only 300 J/kg.  There was 45 to 50 kts of effective shear however.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer was around 500 J/kg, with a freezing level around 12,000 feet.  The parameters were favorable for potential tornado development with the LCL only 750 meters and 50 to 100 J/kg of CAPE in the zero to 3 km layer with steep lapse rates.  Thunderstorms formed in an arc across western Iowa and quickly produced gusty winds and small hail.  The cells tracked north as the line lifted northeast. Numerous reports of tornadoes were received.  The reports were from the same tornado.  Debris was reported with the tornado in Carroll County.  Damage as done in the Lake City area of Calhoun County by a tornado, reported to be 1/4 mile wide by then.  The tornado touched down northwest of Lidderdale and tracked northeast, striking the west side of Lake City. The tornado continued passing through rural Calhoun County and hit some power poles on the west side of Rockwell City before lifting about 2 miles north of Rockwell City in rural Calhoun County. Much of the damage included leaning power poles, tree damage and uprooted trees. In the town of Lake City, the roofing material was removed from the High School, and the roof was removed from a double-wide manufactured home and a warehouse building north of town. The tornado was of EF1 strength with winds of 100 MPH.  The track was 23.5 miles long.  Additional thunderstorms produced hail and high winds.  An 80 MPH wind gust was reported west of Glidden, a machine shed was destroyed in Audubon County, and a weak shed was blown over in Cass County.  Winds were measured at 65 MPH in Pocahontas.  A strong storm moved through the Lake City area following the tornado and produced baseball size hail and destroyed a farm outbuilding west of Lidderdale.

Thunderstorms formed in an arc across western Iowa and quickly produced gusty winds and small hail.  The cells tracked north as the line lifted northeast. Numerous reports of tornadoes were received.  The reports were from the same tornado.  Debris was reported with the tornado in Carroll County.  Damage as done in the Lake City area of Calhoun County by a tornado, reported to be 1/4 mile wide by then.  The tornado touched down northwest of Lidderdale and tracked northeast, striking the west side of Lake City. The tornado continued passing through rural Calhoun County and hit some power poles on the west side of Rockwell City before lifting about 2 miles north of Rockwell City in rural Calhoun County. Much of the damage included leaning power poles, tree damage and uprooted trees. In the town of Lake City, the roofing material was removed from the High School, and the roof was removed from a double-wide manufactured home and a warehouse building north of town. The tornado was of EF1 strength with winds of 100 MPH.  The track was 23.5 miles long.  Additional thunderstorms produced hail and high winds.  An 80 MPH wind gust was reported west of Glidden, a machine shed was destroyed in Audubon County, and a weak shed was blown over in Cass County.  Winds were measured at 65 MPH in Pocahontas.  A strong storm moved through the Lake City area following the tornado and produced baseball size hail and destroyed a farm outbuilding west of Lidderdale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a broad and brief tornado that caused little to no damage. Reported by a spotter and likely track found in satellite data.

With ground already saturated from repeated heavy rainfall events in May and early June, another complex of thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall moved across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa starting during the evening on the 10th and continued into the early morning hours on the 11th. This was followed with additional thunderstorms on the afternoon of the 11th over southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. This additional rainfall led to more significant flooding along area rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor agricultural flooding was observed along both banks of the East Nodaway River near Clarinda. The Iowa Flood Network gauge indicated the river crested nearly 5 feet above flood stage at 20.68 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed throughout the afternoon hours across much of central Iowa as an upper level low moved across the state. Overall the thunderstorms were weak, but parameters were ok for landspout development. A few funnel clouds resulted from the thunderstorms. None of the funnel clouds touched down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small and brief funnel cloud was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS Gages near Riverton on both the West and East Nishnabotna Rivers showed minor flooding with two crests. The first, and more minor of the two crests, came on the 12th and was just 0.3 above food stage on the East Nishnabotna and 0.9 above on the West Nishnabotna. The second higher crests came on the 15th into the 16th and was 0.47 above on the East, and 2.92 feet above on the West. Both crests caused minor low land flooding along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary boundary extended from the Texas Panhandle, across northern Missouri, into southeast Lower Michigan.  Warm air advection continued with an area of elevated thunderstorms developing during the morning hours.  The storms were pulse type severe with the severe threat being hail.  The most unstable CAPE was in the 2000 to 3000 J/kg range, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 700 J/kg.  The atmosphere was moderately sheared with 40 to 45 kts of effective shear available.  One of the storms became severe in the Corning area of Adams County.  The storm produced golf ball size hail.  The storms continued to move east and later produced hail about 1.25 inches in diameter in the Osceola area of Clarke County. As the storms continued to move east, hail around quarter size continues to fall.  The south side of Des Moines received enough hail to completely cover the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large amount of hail was reported.  It was mostly small hail with a few of the larger stones up to half dollar in size.  The hail lasted 5 minutes.

Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon of the 25th as modified gulf moisture returned north into the middle Missouri River Valley. As moisture returned to the area, instability increased and allowed for the thunderstorm development. The majority of the thunderstorms were non-severe in nature due to the weak atmospheric shear and limited instability, but isolated large hail was observed in southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported hail from 1 inch to 1.25 inches in diameter along the path of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved across northern Missouri during the evening of April 7 and into central Illinois during the morning hours of April 8. Severe thunderstorms developed north of this boundary across central Iowa and produced pea to golf ball sized hail along a line from Ottumwa to Burlington Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported half inch to three quarter inch hail was falling at the time of the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening thunderstorm complex moved into western Iowa early in the morning on May 3rd. The light rain falling from the weakening thunderstorms interacted with a strong low-level jet to allow for strong winds to translate to the surface creating strong winds gusts.
A thunderstorm produced nickel size hail at Sioux City in the early evening of April 12th.

A quick moving clipper system impacted eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa during the morning of February 4th. The system traveled southeast through the region along a very tight thermal gradient. The combination of the strong thermal gradient and the clipper system led to strong lift across the area for a short time creating a period of moderate to heavy snowfall. The snowfall impacted the morning commute on the 4th leading to many school closures and accidents on area roadways. Most of the snowfall was over by midday, but north winds of 15 to 25 mph with gusts over 30 mph created some blowing and drifting snowfall into the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 3 to 5 inches across the county including a measured 4.8 inches in Sidney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following dry weather of nearly one month, a period of rather widespread and above normal occurrence of agricultural wildfires corresponded to the period of Fall harvest.  During the period from October 12 through 20th, a combination of very low humidity, strong winds, dry harvested and unharvested fields, and sunshine brought dangerous fire weather conditions.  These conditions allowed many fires to spread to hundreds of acres of farmland before being contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Field fires were reported by emergency management across Scott county, as well as other counties in Eastern Iowa.  These fires impacted roads and extensively burned some already harvested fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of thunderstorms produced several hours of frequent cloud to ground lightning in eastern Iowa the evening of October 20th. The lightning caused several fires in the Iowa City metro area, and caused firefighters to take defensive action against lightning while fighting the structure fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa City's fire department reported that they had three buildings, and one tree set on fire by lightning. Two of the three building fires resulted in fire fighting efforts.  One significant structure fire at 623 E. College St had mutual support called from nearby city departments, and sustained significant fire damage. Fire fighters were forced to take the defensive action of pulling firefighters from ladders, due to the continued frequent lightning in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the hours after midnight on the 28th a low pressure system was over portions of central/eastern Nebraska moving toward Iowa.  A warm front extended east out of the surface low into southern Iowa.  With the progression of the surface low, moderate to heavy rainfall began to enter the state.  Areas that reported the greatest storm total amounts began to see rain around the 2 to 3 am hour and onward through the morning and into the early afternoon as the warm front lingered in central Iowa.  In the end, numerous locations reported total rainfall amounts greater than 4 inches with a handful of reports around 6 inches.  The greater totals were in excess of 9 inches and were recorded in Webster and Hamilton counties, with Dayton in Webster county taking the prize at 9.75 inches. The heavy rainfall allowed many rivers in central Iowa to rise above flood stage. Some of the flooding continued into early September. The entry will be continued in the September 2015 report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Iowa River at Marshalltown crested at 19.98 feet on 31 August 2015 at 8:30 PM CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was making its way across the state from the north and northwest and lit up in the afternoon around 4:30 pm to 5:00 pm just outside of the metro and progressed south and southeastward as isolated to multicell storms.  A tornado formed northwest of Creston, likely aided by interactions of the parent storm with the outflow from a nearby storm.  Otherwise, storms primarily dropped heavy rainfall and quarter sized hail, with a couple reports of golf ball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio report of quarter sized hail in Creston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the hours after midnight on the 28th a low pressure system was over portions of central/eastern Nebraska moving toward Iowa.  A warm front extended east out of the surface low into southern Iowa.  With the progression of the surface low, moderate to heavy rainfall began to enter the state.  Areas that reported the greatest storm total amounts began to see rain around the 2 to 3 am hour and onward through the morning and into the early afternoon as the warm front lingered in central Iowa.  In the end, numerous locations reported total rainfall amounts greater than 4 inches with a handful of reports around 6 inches.  The greater totals were in excess of 9 inches and were recorded in Webster and Hamilton counties, with Dayton in Webster county taking the prize at 9.75 inches. The heavy rainfall allowed many rivers in central Iowa to rise above flood stage. Some of the flooding continued into early September. The entry will be continued in the September 2015 report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 5.40 inches event total with light rain ongoing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front sagged southward through western Iowa and eastern Nebraska during the day on August 2nd. Ahead of the front during the early morning hours the nocturnal low-level jet increased ahead of the front. This allowed for isolated thunderstorms to develop in northeast Nebraska and move into west central Iowa during the early morning hours. Some of these thunderstorms reached severe limits with large hail the main threat. These thunderstorms dissipated in the morning hours, but as the instability increased during the afternoon thunderstorms redeveloped along the cold front. Vertical wind shear also increased during the afternoon as well with some of the thunderstorms becoming supercells producing large hail and damaging winds. The thunderstorms weakened by mid to late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images from social media posts indicate that a downburst occurred in Thurman. Several large tree branches were blown down, and one tree was completely snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moving quickly through the northern Plains allowed for a seasonably strong cold front to sweep through eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa late in the day on the 9th of August. Isolated to scattered supercells developed along the cold front as it moved southeast across the area with some of the supercells producing severe weather in the form of large hail and damaging winds, as well as locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree limbs were reported blown down in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was pushing into the state form the northwest, along which storms initiated in the mid to late afternoon hours.  After the afternoon rainfall, the front wavered/stalled in the area with additional storm development occurring through the evening and overnight hours as the LLJ kicked in. This resulted in heavy rainfall with some totals in excess of 4-5 inches and approaching 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public/citizen weather station reported heavy rainfall of 4.48 inches since 6 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper-level storm system moved across the northern Plains during the day on August 22nd. This system allowed for a strong cold front to sweep across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the late afternoon and evening. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed along the cold front from west central Iowa into eastern Nebraska, and moved southeast through the evening. Although the large majority of these storms were sub-severe, isolated reports of large hail and damaging winds were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media posts from a storm chaser in the area indicated that large street signs had been blown over along highway 34 to the east of Interstate 29. The storm chaser also reported tree damage in the area as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was making its way across the state from the north and northwest and lit up in the afternoon around 4:30 pm to 5:00 pm just outside of the metro and progressed south and southeastward as isolated to multicell storms.  A tornado formed northwest of Creston, likely aided by interactions of the parent storm with the outflow from a nearby storm.  Otherwise, storms primarily dropped heavy rainfall and quarter sized hail, with a couple reports of golf ball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported quarter sized hail NW of Bedford.  Report was relayed via amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought heavy rains to northeast Iowa in the middle of December. Temperatures with this system remained above freezing allowing all the precipitation to fall as rain. Amounts ranged from a little over an inch to almost 5 inches from Winneshiek and Fayette Counties east to the Mississippi River. The highest reported rainfall total in this portion of northeast Iowa was 4.84 inches in Eldorado (Fayette County). This rain caused in bank rises to occur on most rivers and streams along with minor river flooding along the Volga and Turkey Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Turkey River out of its banks in Garber. The river crested at 19.81 feet, almost three feet over the flood stage.

After the afternoon rainfall, the front wavered/stalled in the area with additional storm development occurring through the evening and overnight hours as the LLJ kicked in. This resulted in heavy rainfall with some totals in excess of 4-5 inches and approaching 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public/citizen owned weather station reported heavy rainfall of 3.50 inches since 6 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the hours after midnight on the 28th a low pressure system was over portions of central/eastern Nebraska moving toward Iowa.  A warm front extended east out of the surface low into southern Iowa.  With the progression of the surface low, moderate to heavy rainfall began to enter the state.  Areas that reported the greatest storm total amounts began to see rain around the 2 to 3 am hour and onward through the morning and into the early afternoon as the warm front lingered in central Iowa.  In the end, numerous locations reported total rainfall amounts greater than 4 inches with a handful of reports around 6 inches.  The greater totals were in excess of 9 inches and were recorded in Webster and Hamilton counties, with Dayton in Webster county taking the prize at 9.75 inches. The heavy rainfall allowed many rivers in central Iowa to rise above flood stage. Some of the flooding continued into early September. The entry will be continued in the September 2015 report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: City of Ames alert station reported heavy rainfall of 5.00 inches since 5am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance and its attendant cold front moved east southeastward across the region on the evening of August 2nd. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of this system and became severe producing hail larger than a penny in Keokuk, Scott, and Washington Counties.

A disturbance and its attendant cold front moved east southeastward across the region on the evening of August 2nd. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of this system and became severe producing hail larger than a penny in Keokuk, Scott, and Washington Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of 1.25 inch hail was posted to the Facebook page of KWQC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moving across eastern Iowa on August 8th into the 9th led to the development of showers and thunderstorms that repeatedly moved over the same location and produced excessive rainfall.  Rainfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches were reported in parts of Iowa and Washington Counties. This led to flash flooding that washed out roads in Iowa and Washington Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager for Iowa County reported that several roads remained closed , including F67, due to flash flooding along all forks of the English River and Old Man Creek. A gauge along the North Fork of English River near Parnell crested at 30.0 feet. The river rose above flood stge at 530 AM on August 9 and fell back below flood stage at 6 AM on August 11. Rainfall totals across southwestern Iowa County ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong storm system lifted northward from Iowa into Minnesota on August 18th. This caused a warm front to lift northward across eastern Iowa and warm and moist air to flood into the region. Showers and thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa during the afternoon and evening hours. These thunderstorms produced an EF-0 tornado 3 miles northwest of Stanwood in Cedar County. The storms also produced thunderstorm wind damage in Jones and Des Moines Counties along with hail the size of peas. Torrential rains of 2 to 3 inches caused flash flooding in Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported several trees down in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moving across eastern Iowa on August 8th into the 9th led to the development of showers and thunderstorms that repeatedly moved over the same location and produced excessive rainfall.  Rainfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches were reported in parts of Iowa and Washington Counties. This led to flash flooding that washed out roads in Iowa and Washington Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington County Engineering Departments reported 4 road closures near Kalona due to flash flooding along the English River. A gauge along the English River near Kalona rose above flood stage around 815 AM on August 9 and fell back below flood stage during the afternoon hours of August 12. The river crested at 18.4 feet on August 11.  The county engineer also noted that they begin to see road impacts when the English River reaches 16.5 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex developed ahead of a disturbance that moved across eastern Iowa August 8th to the 9th and produced 3 to 5 inches of rain in the English River basin. This heavy rainfall produced flash flooding that eventually led to the English River near Kalona rising above its major flood stage on August 11th through the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The English River at Kalona rose above the major flood stage of 18 feet during the morning hours of August 11th and fell below 18 feet shortly before 3 AM on August 12.  The river recorded a preliminary crest of 18.48 ft on the 11th.

Showers and thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa during the afternoon and evening hours. These thunderstorms produced an EF-0 tornado 3 miles northwest of Stanwood in Cedar County. The storms also produced thunderstorm wind damage in Jones and Des Moines Counties along with hail the size of peas. Torrential rains of 2 to 3 inches caused flash flooding in Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP Observer reported significant flooding near the intersection of Highway 67 and Highway 30 including the area near Walmart. The flooding occurred immediately following heavy rain. A large enough road area was flooded deep enough that cars were required to avoid it.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported that lightning struck the roof of a home and caused a fire that was quickly extinguished by the fire department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the hours after midnight on the 28th a low pressure system was over portions of central/eastern Nebraska moving toward Iowa.  A warm front extended east out of the surface low into southern Iowa.  With the progression of the surface low, moderate to heavy rainfall began to enter the state.  Areas that reported the greatest storm total amounts began to see rain around the 2 to 3 am hour and onward through the morning and into the early afternoon as the warm front lingered in central Iowa.  In the end, numerous locations reported total rainfall amounts greater than 4 inches with a handful of reports around 6 inches.  The greater totals were in excess of 9 inches and were recorded in Webster and Hamilton counties, with Dayton in Webster county taking the prize at 9.75 inches. The heavy rainfall allowed many rivers in central Iowa to rise above flood stage. Some of the flooding continued into early September. The entry will be continued in the September 2015 report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 6.00 inches event total.  Rain gauge was full at the time as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey rated this as an EF-0 tornado. The peak wind speeds were estimated to be 70 MPH. This tornado touched down in a farm field and no damage was found. The tornado location is based on video evidence provided to us by a storm spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front that was slowly moving eastward across Iowa. Thunderstorms in Jackson and Scott Counties produced damaging winds estimated to be near 60 mph. A thunderstorm in Louisa County caused a fire at a machine shed from a lightning strike. Brief torrential downpours were reported with several of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS Employee reported about 4 to 5 inch diameter tree limbs blown down on the southwest side of town with several smaller branches down. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was making its way across the state from the north and northwest and lit up in the afternoon around 4:30 pm to 5:00 pm just outside of the metro and progressed south and southeastward as isolated to multicell storms.  A tornado formed northwest of Creston, likely aided by interactions of the parent storm with the outflow from a nearby storm.  Otherwise, storms primarily dropped heavy rainfall and quarter sized hail, with a couple reports of golf ball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This segment is for Adams county.  This very visible tornado meandered across Adair and Adams counties for over half an hour, traveling only 8.5 miles in that time. The worst damage occurred near the small hamlet of Williamson where multiple buildings were damaged. The tornado initially traveled to the SW-SE, but executed a cyclonic loop two miles south of Williamson and then traveled to the N-NE for the remainder of its life.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was pushing into the state form the northwest, along which storms initiated in the mid to late afternoon hours.  After the afternoon rainfall, the front wavered/stalled in the area with additional storm development occurring through the evening and overnight hours as the LLJ kicked in. This resulted in heavy rainfall with some totals in excess of 4-5 inches and approaching 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 5.40 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance and its attendant cold front moved east southeastward across the region on the evening of August 2nd. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of this system and became severe producing hail larger than a penny in Keokuk, Scott, and Washington Counties.

In the hours after midnight on the 28th a low pressure system was over portions of central/eastern Nebraska moving toward Iowa.  A warm front extended east out of the surface low into southern Iowa.  With the progression of the surface low, moderate to heavy rainfall began to enter the state.  Areas that reported the greatest storm total amounts began to see rain around the 2 to 3 am hour and onward through the morning and into the early afternoon as the warm front lingered in central Iowa.  In the end, numerous locations reported total rainfall amounts greater than 4 inches with a handful of reports around 6 inches.  The greater totals were in excess of 9 inches and were recorded in Webster and Hamilton counties, with Dayton in Webster county taking the prize at 9.75 inches. The heavy rainfall allowed many rivers in central Iowa to rise above flood stage. Some of the flooding continued into early September. The entry will be continued in the September 2015 report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 4.25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front sagged southward through western Iowa and eastern Nebraska during the day on August 2nd. Ahead of the front during the early morning hours the nocturnal low-level jet increased ahead of the front. This allowed for isolated thunderstorms to develop in northeast Nebraska and move into west central Iowa during the early morning hours. Some of these thunderstorms reached severe limits with large hail the main threat. These thunderstorms dissipated in the morning hours, but as the instability increased during the afternoon thunderstorms redeveloped along the cold front. Vertical wind shear also increased during the afternoon as well with some of the thunderstorms becoming supercells producing large hail and damaging winds. The thunderstorms weakened by mid to late evening.

A shortwave trough moving quickly through the northern Plains allowed for a seasonably strong cold front to sweep through eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa late in the day on the 9th of August. Isolated to scattered supercells developed along the cold front as it moved southeast across the area with some of the supercells producing severe weather in the form of large hail and damaging winds, as well as locally heavy rainfall.

One thunderstorm was severe, and produced damaging winds and large hail up to the size of quarters.  Another thunderstorm produced penny to nickel sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotter reported hail up to the size of quarters, and covered the ground.  Time of the event was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms moved over Iowa, Illinois, and northern Missouri from the early morning hours through the late evening hours. Many locations saw damaging winds during this multiple round event. with locations near the city of Cedar Rapids seeing a very large hail event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Semi trailer reported blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS rolled through northern Iowa in the AM hours bringing with it numerous reports of high/damaging winds and some hail. The damaging winds brought down large tree limbs, power lines, large trees and damaged chicken and hog facilities. Lightning struck a house in Kossuth County damaging the chimney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel to quarter sized hail 2 miles north of Woden.  Report via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms moved over Iowa, Illinois, and northern Missouri from the early morning hours through the late evening hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive damage reported 1 to 2 miles north of Manchester, including farm buildings flattened and trees snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary was pushing through the state with ongoing convection through central Iowa through the day. As the boundary neared the area, the convection intensified and impacted central and southern Iowa in the evening to early morning hours. Two thunderstorm complexes moved through portions of central and southern Iowa with very heavy rainfall causing flash flooding. The severe thunderstorm complexes produced some hail but mainly strong damaging winds. Precipitable water values were near 2 inches in some areas with deep warm cloud depths during the event. This heavy rainfall further exacerbated conditions as some of the area had just received heavy rainfall in previous days. This led to some extended flooding for a few days and flooding on some area rivers. Several locations reported in excess of 4 inches of rain, while one location in Warren County reported over 7.50 inches of rainfall. The Emergency Manager for Marion County mentioned widespread reports of 8 to 10 inches with one report close to 12 inches of accumulated rainfall over the past few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was down on a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS moved into the state in the early morning hours from Minnesota, producing heavy rainfall across large portions of north-central Iowa.  Highest amounts reported were generally in the 4-4.50 range with a 4.60 report in Winnebago county.  The MCS progressed generally S and SSE across central Iowa through the morning hours and was able to strengthen enough by the early afternoon to produce wind damage reports in far south central Iowa, including a few large trees, overturned campers, and downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 20 inch diameter tree down.  Numerous large limbs down as well with torrential rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another morning and early afternoon MCS rolled through Iowa, producing heavy rainfall, a couple of wind damage reports, and a handfull of flash flood reports.  Flash flooding reports were restricted to southern Iowa, while the heavy rain ranged from as far north and west as Webster County and down to Appanoose.  Heavy rainfall totals were generally around 2 inches, but included a 4 inch report from Prairie City in Jasper County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Creston ASOS reported a 61 mph wind gust associated with the storms.

They included the following: Hwy 5 at the Hartford railroad underpass, Hwy 92 at the South River, 20th Ave from Hwy 92 to Jersey St., Kennedy St. just east of 150th Ave with signs of it being washed out and 4 feet of water over the road, 180th and Hwy 92, Hwy S23 and Grimes had 3 feet of water over the road, Interstate 35 between mile marker 57 and 59, Hwy 92 and the railroad overpass by Beech and S31, 165th Ave and Clarke St. had 2 feet over the road, Hwy 92 west of S23, Fulton both at 115th and around S23, G24 from west of R57 to 73rd and from 115th to Hwy 65/69, Idaho St. east of Hwy 28. Hwy 28 on the north edge of Martensdale, S23 north of Hwy 92, and at 165th and Buchanan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of funnel cloud reports were associated with a few weak storms that formed  across central Iowa during the late afternoon. Zero to 3 km CAPE was very strong at around 150 to 200 J/kg with some surface vorticity in place. LCLs were low around 750 m and the Non-Supercell Tornado parameter had a 2 around south central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported to the west of Knoxville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the state late in the afternoon from the north and in doing so a few storms ignited along and near the front.  The storms progressed south and southeast through the evening and into the overnight hours.  A handful of severe reports, hail and wind, came in and were spread out across northern, central, and western Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter to ping pong ball sized hail.  Delayed report, and time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds at a few locations in northwest Iowa on the late evening of July 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including a tree blown down on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and several reports of large hail at scattered locations in northwest Iowa in the late afternoon and evening of August 9th.

A disturbance and its attendant cold front moved east southeastward across the region on the evening of August 2nd. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of this system and became severe producing hail larger than a penny in Keokuk, Scott, and Washington Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Postmaster in Sigourney reported that hail about the size of a golf ball fell a half mile north of town. There were also dents in the siding of a home. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms initiated in the evening and overnight hours across west central and central Iowa.  Training and redevelopment overnight led to a few areas receiving rainfall amounts in excess of 4 inches, including a 7 inch report.  Little to no severe weather otherwise occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported heavy rainfall of 3.70 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding in Sioux City in northwest Iowa during the evening of August 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in a short time caused flash flooding of numerous streets and low areas in Sioux City, with water 2 feet deep in many areas, and manhole cover popping off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front produced areas of thunderstorms with large hail up to the size of quarters and frequent lightning.  One person was killed and another injured by lightning in Muscatine County.  Heavy rain also was common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported several stones of dime to nickel sized. Most stones were less than 0.5 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front produced areas of thunderstorms with large hail up to the size of quarters and frequent lightning.  One person was killed and another injured by lightning in Muscatine County.  Heavy rain also was common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water around 1 foot deep covered Mississippi Drive, as confirmed through Muscatine police, a spotter and also the Muscatine Journal Star Newspaper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure area was located over eastern Colorado during the afternoon of the 18th.  Moisture streamed north ahead of it with marginal instability over the state.  CAPE rose to around 1000 J/kg by mid afternoon.  The non-severe tornado index was elevated from southeast into parts of central and north central Iowa.  The freezing level was quite low, around 10,000 feet, however the CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was only around 200 J/kg.  Thunderstorms formed in the afternoon and though most remained below severe levels, there were a few severe reports.  One storm produced quarter size hail that covered the ground in Decatur County.  Another storm produced an apparent microburst that destroyed a machine shed and blew it 600 feet into a field in Marshall County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail covered Interstate 35.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the afternoon storms fired up ahead of and along a cold front that was entering Iowa from the northwest.  Numerous storms had severe hail, produced damaging winds, and two tornadoes. The first tornado was on the ground for several miles tracking through portions of Marion, Lucas and Monroe Counties. The second tornado formed just west of Albia and struck the town of Albia damaging several homes and businesses. Flash flooding was also reported in Davis and Monroe Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 18 inch diameter trees snapped.  Wind gust lasted from approximately 6:50pm to 7:20pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across much of central and southern Iowa toward the latter portion of the month leading to flooding along many of the rivers across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Racoon River at Des Moines Hwy 28 crested at 40.36 feet at 1:15 PM CST on 26 June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains fell across the Raccoon and Des Moines River Basin near and upstream of the Des Moines area leading to a period of flooding at locations within the Des Moines Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river crested at 26.61 feet at 11:00 AM CST on 15 June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight and morning rainfall was heavy, totally in excess of 2 inches in many locations, including high values of 4.2 and 6.4 respectively in the State Center area.  A couple instances of flash flooding were observed as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Updated 24 hour rainfall amount of approximately 6.40 inches reported by the county Emergency Manager.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front pushed into southern Iowa from Missouri, bringing with it storms and heavy rainfall.  The front progressed slowly, aiding in keeping the rain over the same areas for long periods of time.  By the end of the day, a number of areas across the south reported 4+ inches of rain with the highest totals around 5.5-5.75 near Lorimor, Rathbun, Derby, and Humeston.  Localized flash flooding ensued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 2.62 inches of rain since about 2 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A primarily heavy rain event affecting the southern half of Iowa. Storms were ongoing across portions of central Iowa during the day, with an area of low pressure centered across north central Kansas. Additional storm development occurred across southern Iowa to the north of a stationary front draped eastward from the surface low across northern Missouri. A few severe reports occurred with some large hail reported in Taylor County and damaging winds across portions of Taylor, Appanoose and Davis Counties. Flash flooding was also reported with the heavy rainfall in Taylor County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 24 hour rainfall total of 2 inches near Ames High School.  Reported via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was pushing southward through the state this day, with little action to speak of through much of the state.  By the afternoon, a few storms were able to fire up along/ahead of the cold front and produced a couple of isolated severe weather reports, hail and damaging winds, in far southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor roof damage to shingles and soffit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the state during the day, dragging behind it a cold front that helped trigger off storms across portions of central and southern Iowa. Hail, damaging winds and a tornado occurred with the storms. The tornado moved from Monroe County into Wapello County damaging multiple farmsteads, power poles and a house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of golf ball sized hail with photos taken from the KCCI ulocal page.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the hours after midnight on the 28th a low pressure system was over portions of central/eastern Nebraska moving toward Iowa.  A warm front extended east out of the surface low into southern Iowa.  With the progression of the surface low, moderate to heavy rainfall began to enter the state.  Areas that reported the greatest storm total amounts began to see rain around the 2 to 3 am hour and onward through the morning and into the early afternoon as the warm front lingered in central Iowa.  In the end, numerous locations reported total rainfall amounts greater than 4 inches with a handful of reports around 6 inches.  The greater totals were in excess of 9 inches and were recorded in Webster and Hamilton counties, with Dayton in Webster county taking the prize at 9.75 inches. The heavy rainfall allowed many rivers in central Iowa to rise above flood stage. Some of the flooding continued into early September. The entry will be continued in the September 2015 report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local Law Enforcement reported many county roads were closed due to water over the road or the road having been washed out. A bridge was washed out at the 2900 block of East 110th between U and T Avenues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought heavy rains to northeast Iowa in the middle of December. Temperatures with this system remained above freezing allowing all the precipitation to fall as rain. Amounts ranged from a little over an inch to almost 5 inches from Winneshiek and Fayette Counties east to the Mississippi River. The highest reported rainfall total in this portion of northeast Iowa was 4.84 inches in Eldorado (Fayette County). This rain caused in bank rises to occur on most rivers and streams along with minor river flooding along the Volga and Turkey Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 3.84 inches of rain fell.  Other totals in the county included 3.03 at Elkader 6SSW and 2.80 inches near Strawberry Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms moved over Iowa, Illinois, and northern Missouri from the early morning hours through the late evening hours. Many locations saw damaging winds during this multiple round event. with locations near the city of Cedar Rapids seeing a very large hail event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter's windshield was broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms moved over Iowa, Illinois, and northern Missouri from the early morning hours through the late evening hours. Many locations saw damaging winds during this multiple round event. with locations near the city of Cedar Rapids seeing a very large hail event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail and heavy rain was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved in from the west through the day, sparking off storms along the way.  The vast majority produced periods of heavy rainfall and sub-severe hail, with only a lone severe hail report of quarter size out of Davis County during the evening of the 17th.  Storms lingered into the early hours of the 18th, dropping sub-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front entered the state and produced heavy rainfall across a large portion of central Iowa.  The front eventually stalled, and transitioned to a northward moving warm front the next day.  Rainfall totals were generally in excess of 2 inches, with the largest reports in excess of 5 inches, resulting in some localized flash flooding.  In addition to the heavy rainfall, a lone severe hail report came early in the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 2.70 inches storm total thus far.

An area of upper level low pressure slowly moved across the central Plains during the September 22nd through the 23rd time frame. Significant moisture was in place across the mid Missouri River Valley ahead of this system. This combination led to scattered to numerous thunderstorms over eastern Nebraska and western Iowa starting on the 22nd and continuing into the day on the 23rd. The heavy persistent thunderstorms, especially late in the night and into the early morning hours on the 23rd led to significant flash flooding across parts of western Iowa, specifically in Council Bluffs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of flash flooding were received from broadcast media, spotters, and social media. Very heavy rainfall occurred during the overnight and into the early morning hours on the 23rd with the Council Bluffs area seeing over 7 inches of rainfall. This lead to significant flash flooding of streets and small streams. This had a large impact on the morning rush hour with numerous cars stalled with several water rescues taking place. Basement flooding also occurred throughout the city during the morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strongest outbreak of cold air this season so far swept into the state during the evening of the 6th and held its grip into the early morning hours of the 7th.  A strong Arctic cold front moved through the state in the wake of the fresh snowfall that occurred on the 5th.  Temperatures fell to between zero and 10 below zero F over a large part of the state.  The winds were a major factor with northwest winds sustained between 20 and 30 MPH with gusts in the 40 to 45 MPH range.  The combination of winds and sub-zero temperatures produced wind chill values of 30 to near 40 below zero from the early morning hours of the 6th into the early morning hours of the 7th.  Winds became west and diminished during the evening of the 6th, though temperatures fell to around -10 by that time.  It wasn't until after midnight that winds diminished to the point that wind chill values were in the -20's.

A major winter storm affected the entire state during the evening of 31 January into the evening of the 1 February.  The northern and southern branch of the jet stream phased over the central U.S.  The southern branch drew tropical moisture north into the system as the northern branch brought down colder air.  Low pressure organized over the southwest U.S. and moved into Kansas during the evening of the 31st, then tracked across Missouri and into Indiana on the evening of the 1st. Initially, a frontal boundary extended east-northeast out of the low pressure across Iowa.  The precipitation fell as rain initially over much of the state.  As colder air moved in from the north and west, the rain changed over to heavy wet snow.  An extended period of snowfall of a half inch to an inch per hour occurred.  Roads became hazardous with some even impassible. Numerous closures occurred.  It was fortunate that the storm occurred on a weekend, which decreased the impact somewhat.  During the overnight hours, a band of heavy snow set up across central into east central Iowa.  These areas received in excess of a foot of snow.  This area was generally from west of Des Moines, through Cedar Rapids, into the Quad Cities area.  South of the band the snow amounts were generally in the 8 to 10 inch range as the dry slot from the system slowed snowfall rates.  North of the band total snowfall was generally around 8 inches.  The heavy consistency of the snow during the evening of the 31st into the early morning of the 1st caused power outages as the snow stuck to tree branches.  At the peak of the storm, about 16,700 people were without power.  As the storm progressed east, winds increased from the north.  Winds of 20 to 35 MPH were common, with some gusts of 45 to 50 MPH observed on the 1st.  The snow had become drier at that time and was more easily blown around.  Blowing snow caused whiteout conditions at times with considerable drifting of the snow.  Some of the heaviest snow fell over the central Iowa area.  The NWS office in Johnston recorded 14.2 inches, Madrid in Boone County received 14.1 inches, 13.7 inches was reported in Saylorville and Ankeny in Polk County, with 13 inches in Altoona in Polk County.  Cold air overspread the state with morning lows on the 2nd falling to zero to 15 below zero.  The storm beginning time actually occurred on the 31st of January, though criteria was not met until the 1st of February.  The snow started west of an Algona to Fort Dodge to Creston line by 1500 CST on the 31st, reached a Waterloo to Centerville line by 1900 CST on the 31st, and covered the entire CWA by 2100 CST on the 31st. There was one death from the storm.  Police in Cass County reported that a woman died of exposure Sunday evening when attempting to get help after crashing her car. A female age 67 lost control and crashed into a ditch around 1630 CST Sunday near Atlantic.  She left her car and tried to walk home. Her body was found about four hours later a few blocks from her car. The wind chill was 9 F below zero at the time and she was reportedly not dressed for the weather.

Low pressure organized over the southwest U.S. and moved into Kansas during the evening of the 31st, then tracked across Missouri and into Indiana on the evening of the 1st. Initially, a frontal boundary extended east-northeast out of the low pressure across Iowa.  The precipitation fell as rain initially over much of the state.  As colder air moved in from the north and west, the rain changed over to heavy wet snow.  An extended period of snowfall of a half inch to an inch per hour occurred.  Roads became hazardous with some even impassible. Numerous closures occurred.  It was fortunate that the storm occurred on a weekend, which decreased the impact somewhat.  During the overnight hours, a band of heavy snow set up across central into east central Iowa.  These areas received in excess of a foot of snow.  This area was generally from west of Des Moines, through Cedar Rapids, into the Quad Cities area.  South of the band the snow amounts were generally in the 8 to 10 inch range as the dry slot from the system slowed snowfall rates.  North of the band total snowfall was generally around 8 inches.  The heavy consistency of the snow during the evening of the 31st into the early morning of the 1st caused power outages as the snow stuck to tree branches.  At the peak of the storm, about 16,700 people were without power.  As the storm progressed east, winds increased from the north.  Winds of 20 to 35 MPH were common, with some gusts of 45 to 50 MPH observed on the 1st.  The snow had become drier at that time and was more easily blown around.  Blowing snow caused whiteout conditions at times with considerable drifting of the snow.  Some of the heaviest snow fell over the central Iowa area.  The NWS office in Johnston recorded 14.2 inches, Madrid in Boone County received 14.1 inches, 13.7 inches was reported in Saylorville and Ankeny in Polk County, with 13 inches in Altoona in Polk County.  Cold air overspread the state with morning lows on the 2nd falling to zero to 15 below zero.  The storm beginning time actually occurred on the 31st of January, though criteria was not met until the 1st of February.  The snow started west of an Algona to Fort Dodge to Creston line by 1500 CST on the 31st, reached a Waterloo to Centerville line by 1900 CST on the 31st, and covered the entire CWA by 2100 CST on the 31st. There was one death from the storm.  Police in Cass County reported that a woman died of exposure Sunday evening when attempting to get help after crashing her car. A female age 67 lost control and crashed into a ditch around 1630 CST Sunday near Atlantic.  She left her car and tried to walk home. Her body was found about four hours later a few blocks from her car. The wind chill was 9 F below zero at the time and she was reportedly not dressed for the weather.

Thunderstorms produced large hail at a few locations in extreme  northwest Iowa during the late afternoon of September 9th. There was also one report of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail caused an unknown amount of vehicle damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail at a few locations in extreme  northwest Iowa during the late afternoon of September 9th. There was also one report of heavy rainfall.

A slow moving cold front entered the state and produced heavy rainfall across a large portion of central Iowa.  The front eventually stalled, and transitioned to a northward moving warm front the next day.  Rainfall totals were generally in excess of 2 inches, with the largest reports in excess of 5 inches, resulting in some localized flash flooding.  In addition to the heavy rainfall, a lone severe hail report came early in the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 2.25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm hit northeast Iowa on December 28th and 29th with heavy snow, sleet and strong winds.  As the storm lifted north out of the Southern Plains states, snow moved into northeast Iowa during the morning of the 28th. For parts of northeast Iowa, the snow was mixed with or completely changed over to sleet during the afternoon and early evening of the 28th. The heaviest snow fell during the late afternoon and early evening of the 28th with 1 to 2 inch per hour rates. This caused a rapid deterioration in road conditions causing travel conditions to quickly become dangerous and some county sheriff departments recommended no travel.  By the time the snow ended on the 29th, totals across northeast Iowa ranged from 6 to 11 inches. The highest reported total was 11.5 inches in Monona (Clayton County). Significant blowing and drifting of the snow occurred as winds gusted into the 25 to 40 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 5 to 7 inches of snow across Fayette County. The snow mixed with or completely changed to sleet for a while during the afternoon and early evening of the 28th. The highest reported total was 7.3 inches near Oelwein. Winds gusting into the 35 to 50 mph range created considerable blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strongest outbreak of cold air this season so far swept into the state during the evening of the 6th and held its grip into the early morning hours of the 7th.  A strong Arctic cold front moved through the state in the wake of the fresh snowfall that occurred on the 5th.  Temperatures fell to between zero and 10 below zero F over a large part of the state.  The winds were a major factor with northwest winds sustained between 20 and 30 MPH with gusts in the 40 to 45 MPH range.  The combination of winds and sub-zero temperatures produced wind chill values of 30 to near 40 below zero from the early morning hours of the 6th into the early morning hours of the 7th.  Winds became west and diminished during the evening of the 6th, though temperatures fell to around -10 by that time.  It wasn't until after midnight that winds diminished to the point that wind chill values were in the -20's.

As colder air moved in from the north and west, the rain changed over to heavy wet snow.  An extended period of snowfall of a half inch to an inch per hour occurred.  Roads became hazardous with some even impassible. Numerous closures occurred.  It was fortunate that the storm occurred on a weekend, which decreased the impact somewhat.  During the overnight hours, a band of heavy snow set up across central into east central Iowa.  These areas received in excess of a foot of snow.  This area was generally from west of Des Moines, through Cedar Rapids, into the Quad Cities area.  South of the band the snow amounts were generally in the 8 to 10 inch range as the dry slot from the system slowed snowfall rates.  North of the band total snowfall was generally around 8 inches.  The heavy consistency of the snow during the evening of the 31st into the early morning of the 1st caused power outages as the snow stuck to tree branches.  At the peak of the storm, about 16,700 people were without power.  As the storm progressed east, winds increased from the north.  Winds of 20 to 35 MPH were common, with some gusts of 45 to 50 MPH observed on the 1st.  The snow had become drier at that time and was more easily blown around.  Blowing snow caused whiteout conditions at times with considerable drifting of the snow.  Some of the heaviest snow fell over the central Iowa area.  The NWS office in Johnston recorded 14.2 inches, Madrid in Boone County received 14.1 inches, 13.7 inches was reported in Saylorville and Ankeny in Polk County, with 13 inches in Altoona in Polk County.  Cold air overspread the state with morning lows on the 2nd falling to zero to 15 below zero.  The storm beginning time actually occurred on the 31st of January, though criteria was not met until the 1st of February.  The snow started west of an Algona to Fort Dodge to Creston line by 1500 CST on the 31st, reached a Waterloo to Centerville line by 1900 CST on the 31st, and covered the entire CWA by 2100 CST on the 31st. There was one death from the storm.  Police in Cass County reported that a woman died of exposure Sunday evening when attempting to get help after crashing her car. A female age 67 lost control and crashed into a ditch around 1630 CST Sunday near Atlantic.  She left her car and tried to walk home. Her body was found about four hours later a few blocks from her car. The wind chill was 9 F below zero at the time and she was reportedly not dressed for the weather.

A slow moving cold front entered the state and produced heavy rainfall across a large portion of central Iowa.  The front eventually stalled, and transitioned to a northward moving warm front the next day.  Rainfall totals were generally in excess of 2 inches, with the largest reports in excess of 5 inches, resulting in some localized flash flooding.  In addition to the heavy rainfall, a lone severe hail report came early in the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported Highway 25 underpass flooded in the town of Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the region producing scattered thunderstorms along and behind the front.  Isolated hail occurred with the strongest storms in a couple of locations.
A cold front moved across the region producing scattered thunderstorms along and behind the front.  Isolated hail occurred with the strongest storms in a couple of locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime sized hail was observed for 1 minute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong warm air advection took place across Iowa during the day on the 5th.  The airmass was quite cold with shallow Arctic air in place.  At the same time a weak surface front pushed southeast out of Nebraska into northern Missouri.  This was a fairly typical Alberta Clipper type weather system.  Snow began to fall heavily around mid day and lasted through the evening.  A band of heavy snow fell from northwest of Fort Dodge through Oskaloosa.  In the band, six to nearly 8 inches of snow fell in a 12 hour period.  The heaviest snow fell in Ankeny, where 7.8 inches was measured.  Around 7.5 inches fell in the Ames area.  There was little wind associated with the storm, however road conditions became quite slick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strongest outbreak of cold air this season so far swept into the state during the evening of the 6th and held its grip into the early morning hours of the 7th.  A strong Arctic cold front moved through the state in the wake of the fresh snowfall that occurred on the 5th.  Temperatures fell to between zero and 10 below zero F over a large part of the state.  The winds were a major factor with northwest winds sustained between 20 and 30 MPH with gusts in the 40 to 45 MPH range.  The combination of winds and sub-zero temperatures produced wind chill values of 30 to near 40 below zero from the early morning hours of the 6th into the early morning hours of the 7th.  Winds became west and diminished during the evening of the 6th, though temperatures fell to around -10 by that time.  It wasn't until after midnight that winds diminished to the point that wind chill values were in the -20's.

The winds were a major factor with northwest winds sustained between 20 and 30 MPH with gusts in the 40 to 45 MPH range.  The combination of winds and sub-zero temperatures produced wind chill values of 30 to near 40 below zero from the early morning hours of the 6th into the early morning hours of the 7th.  Winds became west and diminished during the evening of the 6th, though temperatures fell to around -10 by that time.  It wasn't until after midnight that winds diminished to the point that wind chill values were in the -20's.

Strong warm air advection took place across Iowa during the day on the 5th.  The airmass was quite cold with shallow Arctic air in place.  At the same time a weak surface front pushed southeast out of Nebraska into northern Missouri.  This was a fairly typical Alberta Clipper type weather system.  Snow began to fall heavily around mid day and lasted through the evening.  A band of heavy snow fell from northwest of Fort Dodge through Oskaloosa.  In the band, six to nearly 8 inches of snow fell in a 12 hour period.  The heaviest snow fell in Ankeny, where 7.8 inches was measured.  Around 7.5 inches fell in the Ames area.  There was little wind associated with the storm, however road conditions became quite slick.

Very hot temperatures started the month of September across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. As the upper level ridge of high pressure started to break down as a storm system moved across the northern Plains, a cold front moved into the area. With the hot and humid conditions ahead of the front, strong instability developed across the area. Thunderstorms developed along the front during the late afternoon and continued into the overnight hours. Some of these storms were severe producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Is there damage reported.
